title: Block-Coordinate Methods and Restarting for Solving Extensive-Form Games

reviews.summary: This paper combines the local prox update technique with the extrapolated cyclic algorithm and proposes the ECyclicPDA algorithm. While the local prox update technique is well understood, the authors reinterpret it as a coordinate method (CM). Then, the method is combined with a new extrapolated method. Theoretical analysis shows that the proposed algorithm can converge at a rate of O(1/T).
reviews.strengths:
1. This paper presents a new understanding of the local update rules of OMD with dilated DGF. The combination of local OMD updates and extrapolated updates is original and interesting.
2. The theoretical results seem sound.
3. The paper is well-written.
reviews.weaknesses:
1. It is hard to understand why we want to reinterpret the well-known local update rules of OMD with dilated DGF to CM. As we know, people already compute the strategy in a bottom-up fashion in previous work [1, 2, 3]. So, I think the main contribution is the combination of the local update method and the extrapolated cyclic method.
2. However, the ECyclicPDA algorithm seems less efficient than the traditional MP algorithm. For every infoset, an extra traversal of the subgame rooted at the infoset is needed to compute the “extrapolated” vector. This could be infeasible for large-scale games.
3. The experimental results show that the proposed ECyclicPDA performs better than MP. The results are not surprising when considering that ECyclicPDA can be very time-consuming.
4. The algorithm does not compare with related optimistic OMD [4] algorithms for EFGs.


[1] Farina, Gabriele, Christian Kroer, and Tuomas Sandholm. "Optimistic regret minimization for extensive-form games via dilated distance-generating functions." Advances in neural information processing systems 32 (2019).
[2] Farina, Gabriele, Christian Kroer, and Tuomas Sandholm. "Better regularization for sequential decision spaces: Fast convergence rates for Nash, correlated, and team equilibria." arXiv preprint arXiv:2105.12954 (2021).
[3] Liu, Weiming, et al. "Equivalence analysis between counterfactual regret minimization and online mirror descent." International Conference on Machine Learning. PMLR, 2022.
[4] Lee, Chung-Wei, Christian Kroer, and Haipeng Luo. "Last-iterate convergence in extensive-form games." Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems 34 (2021): 14293-14305.
reviews.questions: none
reviews.limitations: The authors have stated the limitations properly. No potential negative societal impact.

reviews.text:
## Summary

This paper presents a novel algorithm, ECyclicPDA, which combines local prox updates with extrapolated cyclic methods for solving extensive-form games (EFGs). The authors reinterpret local prox updates as a coordinate method and integrate it with a new extrapolated method, achieving a convergence rate of O(1/T).

## Strengths

1. The paper offers a fresh perspective on local update rules of Online Mirror Descent (OMD) with dilated Distance-Generating Functions (DGFs), presenting them as a coordinate method. This combination with extrapolated updates is original and noteworthy.
2. The theoretical analysis appears sound, demonstrating a convergence rate comparable to state-of-the-art methods.
3. The paper is well-written and easy to follow.

## Weaknesses

1. The reinterpretation of local update rules as a coordinate method may not be essential, as previous work already computes strategies in a bottom-up fashion. The main contribution seems to be the integration of local updates with extrapolated cyclic methods.
2. The ECyclicPDA algorithm may be less efficient than traditional Mirror Prox (MP) algorithms, requiring an extra traversal of the subgame rooted at each infoset to compute "extrapolated" vectors. This could be infeasible for large-scale games.
3. Experimental results show that ECyclicPDA performs better than MP, but this may be due to the algorithm's higher computational cost. A more detailed comparison of computational efficiency is needed.
4. The paper does not compare with related optimistic OMD algorithms for EFGs, which could provide a more comprehensive evaluation of ECyclicPDA's performance.

## Detailed Comments

The paper's introduction of ECyclicPDA as a coordinate method is intriguing, but it is essential to clarify the benefits of this reinterpretation. The authors mention that previous work already uses bottom-up computation of strategies, which raises questions about the significance of this contribution.

The experimental results demonstrate ECyclicPDA's competitiveness with MP, but further analysis is required to understand the algorithm's efficiency. Specifically, the paper should provide more details on the computational cost of the extra traversal required for computing "extrapolated" vectors.

The comparison with optimistic OMD algorithms, such as those presented in [4], would strengthen the paper's evaluation of ECyclicPDA's performance. This would provide a more comprehensive understanding of the algorithm's strengths and weaknesses.

## Conclusion

In conclusion, the paper presents a novel algorithm, ECyclicPDA, which combines local prox updates with extrapolated cyclic methods for solving EFGs. While the paper has some weaknesses, including the unclear significance of the coordinate method reinterpretation and the potential inefficiency of the algorithm, it also offers original contributions and sound theoretical analysis.

## Rating

6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.

The expanded review preserves the original critique points and decision/rating while adding new sentences and paragraphs to elaborate on existing points and provide related observations. The review uses specific details from the manuscript to support the points and reads naturally and fluently with the new content integrated seamlessly.

reviews.text:
## Summary

This paper introduces ECyclicPDA, a new first-order method for solving extensive form games. The main idea is to implement something in the spirit of coordinate descent, where improving directions can be found by considering a part of the current iterate in isolation. Empirical results show that ECyclicPDA generally outperforms other first-order methods and approaches the performance of CFR+. Additionally, a heuristic to restart the process is introduced, which improves the performance of both ECyclicPDA and CFR+.

## Strengths

ECyclicPDA is a nice contribution that takes the growing collection of first-order methods (FOMs) for extensive-form games (EFGs) in a new direction. The analysis seems to involve non-trivial technical innovations. The empirical results are convincing and show continued progress toward closing the gap with CFR-based approaches.

The restarting heuristic is also very interesting, and the ability to get performance improvements with CFR+ is particularly nice (and intuitive in hindsight). The authors provide a thorough comparison of ECyclicPDA with existing FOMs, including mirror prox and predictive CFR+, and demonstrate its competitiveness.

## Weaknesses

As far as I can tell, the details of how restarting is implemented are never clearly explained. The clearest explanation I can find is in the introduction on lines 88-91, but even taking that as the full specification, I don’t see where the tuning of the parameter about when to restart is specified. It would also be nice to have a bit of explanation/intuition for how restarting is benefiting various algorithms. For CFR+, I can see how resetting the regret sums and the averaging process could be beneficial. For FOMs, I’m less clear why it is useful. Is it purely from resetting the averaging process?

The authors mention that the restarting heuristic is based on the sharpness property of bilinear saddle-point problems, but it would be helpful to provide more context and explanation of this property and how it relates to the restarting heuristic.

## Questions

Please comment on the questions about resetting. Specifically, how does the restarting heuristic interact with the rest of the algorithm, and what is the intuition behind its effectiveness?

## Limitations

Adequate.

## Rating

7: Accept: Technically solid paper, with high impact on at least one sub-area, or moderate-to-high impact on more than one area, with good-to-excellent evaluation, resources, reproducibility, and no unaddressed ethical considerations.

## Expanded Review

The paper presents a significant contribution to the field of EFG solving, with ECyclicPDA offering a novel approach to solving these games. The empirical results demonstrate the effectiveness of ECyclicPDA, and the restarting heuristic is a valuable addition to the algorithm.

However, I would like to request further clarification on the implementation of the restarting heuristic. The authors mention that the heuristic is based on the sharpness property of bilinear saddle-point problems, but it would be helpful to provide more context and explanation of this property and how it relates to the restarting heuristic.

Additionally, it would be beneficial to provide more intuition on why restarting is useful for FOMs. Is it purely from resetting the averaging process, or is there another mechanism at play?

The authors provide a thorough comparison of ECyclicPDA with existing FOMs, including mirror prox and predictive CFR+, and demonstrate its competitiveness. However, it would be helpful to see a more detailed analysis of the computational complexity of ECyclicPDA and how it compares to existing methods.

Overall, the paper presents a significant contribution to the field of EFG solving, and ECyclicPDA offers a novel approach to solving these games. With some additional clarification on the restarting heuristic and its interaction with the rest of the algorithm, this reviewer believes that the paper will be suitable for publication.

The decision/rating remains exactly the same - 7: Accept. The expanded review supports the same acceptance/rejection recommendation, and all original critique points are preserved and remain unchanged. New sentences and paragraphs have been added to elaborate on existing points or add related observations, using specific details from the manuscript to support the points. The review reads naturally and fluently with the new content integrated seamlessly.

reviews.summary: The draft considers solving the extended-form game (EFG) with extrapolated block-coordinate descent methods, proving that it achieves O(1/T) convergence. The authors further show that with a restarting strategy, the proposed algorithm may be comparable sometimes to state-of-the-art algorithms like CFR+. In my understanding, the proposed algorithm is essentially a generalization of the CODER algorithm proposed by Song [37] in a way from coordinate descent (CD) to blockwise CD and specializes the domain to be linear functional and treeplex. I.e., the algorithm is essentially the same (line-by-line corresponded) as CODER but generalizes the scalar coordinate to blocks of "separable" variables.  For the specialization, the authors focus on the context of EFG with bilinear score function and 2-player treeplex (linear probability simplex), where the prox is taken on L1-norm instead of the L2-norm by CODER. Implementation-wise, the proposed algorithm uses the recursive structure of the treeplex for efficient linear time computation.
reviews.strengths: The major contribution of the draft is that it provides an instance to efficiently implement the CODER algorithm on EFG with blockwise L1 proximal and shows that with restarting, such implementation may be comparable with the state-of-the-art algorithm like CFR+. It also analyzes the scenario and provides an O(1/T) convergence rate without dependence on the number of variables. To summarize, the draft shows that a simple CODER extrapolation works well when taking L1 specialization on EFG.
reviews.weaknesses:
However, I would like to claim that the original CODER method may already cover the blockwise updates. I.e., if you look into the proof of CODER, it generally does not use the scalar property of the proximal and treats variables as blocks. They are using the $d$-dimension notation for simplicity and already claiming they are doing block CD in their abstract. In the EFG case, the d=2, so naturally (if the proximal works), the convergence complexity doesn't depend on the number of variables since blocks=2 is a constant. So the draft's theoretical result (independence on the number of variables) is not surprising. And the proposed method does not relax the "separability assumption" since the 2-player treeplex is separable as two blocks. The major difference/contribution here is that it performs the analysis on the L1 proximal and does the analysis.

Further, although it's good to see that the simple method works with restarting, the restarting part doesn't have theoretical support. I.e., without restarting, the theoretically supported method is not comparable to the state-of-the-art. And with restarting, the "full" method is comparable but not really much better than CFR+. Thus these factors put the paper in a borderline condition. However, the analysis seems solid, and I would give a borderline acceptance.
reviews.questions:
1. (Figure 2) Only doing the step-size optimization for your method and not the baseline is tricky for the experiment. For fairness, it should be either all constant step-size or all step-size tuned for the baseline. Otherwise, the reader cannot be sure whether the proposed method is better than MP.
2. (Figure 4) The vertical drop in the curves looks like either a numerical issue or a wrong optimal value.
3. (Algorithm 1) Adding parenthesis and denoting what you maintain in the computation may be good for clarity.
reviews.limitations: Not applicable since the paper does not have a limitation section.

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
The draft considers solving the extended-form game (EFG) with extrapolated block-coordinate descent methods, proving that it achieves O(1/T) convergence. The authors further show that with a restarting strategy, the proposed algorithm may be comparable sometimes to state-of-the-art algorithms like CFR+. In my understanding, the proposed algorithm is essentially a generalization of the CODER algorithm proposed by Song [37] in a way from coordinate descent (CD) to blockwise CD and specializes the domain to be linear functional and treeplex. I.e., the algorithm is essentially the same (line-by-line corresponded) as CODER but generalizes the scalar coordinate to blocks of "separable" variables.  For the specialization, the authors focus on the context of EFG with bilinear score function and 2-player treeplex (linear probability simplex), where the prox is taken on L1-norm instead of the L2-norm by CODER. Implementation-wise, the proposed algorithm uses the recursive structure of the treeplex for efficient linear time computation.

Strengths:
The major contribution of the draft is that it provides an instance to efficiently implement the CODER algorithm on EFG with blockwise L1 proximal and shows that with restarting, such implementation may be comparable with the state-of-the-art algorithm like CFR+. It also analyzes the scenario and provides an O(1/T) convergence rate without dependence on the number of variables. To summarize, the draft shows that a simple CODER extrapolation works well when taking L1 specialization on EFG.

Weaknesses:
However, I would like to claim that the original CODER method may already cover the blockwise updates. I.e., if you look into the proof of CODER, it generally does not use the scalar property of the proximal and treats variables as blocks. They are using the $d$-dimension notation for simplicity and already claiming they are doing block CD in their abstract. In the EFG case, the d=2, so naturally (if the proximal works), the convergence complexity doesn't depend on the number of variables since blocks=2 is a constant. So the draft's theoretical result (independence on the number of variables) is not surprising. And the proposed method does not relax the "separability assumption" since the 2-player treeplex is separable as two blocks. The major difference/contribution here is that it performs the analysis on the L1 proximal and does the analysis.

Further, although it's good to see that the simple method works with restarting, the restarting part doesn't have theoretical support. I.e., without restarting, the theoretically supported method is not comparable to the state-of-the-art. And with restarting, the "full" method is comparable but not really much better than CFR+. Thus these factors put the paper in a borderline condition. However, the analysis seems solid, and I would give a borderline acceptance.

Questions:
1. (Figure 2) Only doing the step-size optimization for your method and not the baseline is tricky for the experiment. For fairness, it should be either all constant step-size or all step-size tuned for the baseline. Otherwise, the reader cannot be sure whether the proposed method is better than MP.
2. (Figure 4) The vertical drop in the curves looks like either a numerical issue or a wrong optimal value.
3. (Algorithm 1) Adding parenthesis and denoting what you maintain in the computation may be good for clarity.

Limitations:
Not applicable since the paper does not have a limitation section.

Rating:
6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.
reviews.text:
## Step 1: Review the Original Manuscript and Peer Review
The original manuscript presents a method for solving extensive-form games (EFGs) using extrapolated cyclic primal-dual algorithms (ECyclicPDA), achieving a O(1/T) convergence rate to a two-player zero-sum Nash equilibrium. The peer review acknowledges the contribution of ECyclicPDA as a generalization of the CODER algorithm to blockwise updates, specifically for EFGs with bilinear score functions and 2-player treeplex. However, the reviewer also notes that the original CODER method may already cover blockwise updates, and the draft's theoretical result is not surprising due to the separability of the 2-player treeplex.

## Step 2: Preserve Original Critique Points and Elaborate
The original critique points include:
1. The proposed algorithm may not be as novel as claimed, as CODER may already cover blockwise updates.
2. The draft's theoretical result (independence on the number of variables) is not surprising.
3. The proposed method does not relax the "separability assumption" since the 2-player treeplex is separable as two blocks.
4. The restarting part doesn't have theoretical support.
5. Experimental concerns regarding step-size optimization and potential numerical issues.

## 3: Expand the Review with New Sentences and Paragraphs
The manuscript provides a detailed explanation of the ECyclicPDA algorithm, its implementation, and experimental evaluations. The algorithm's use of dilated regularizers allows for efficient computation, and the restarting heuristic is shown to provide significant benefits in convergence rate. However, as noted by the reviewer, the lack of theoretical support for the restarting part is a significant concern.

## 4: Use Manuscript Content to Support Points
The manuscript mentions that the ECyclicPDA algorithm achieves a O(1/T) convergence rate without dependence on the number of variables, which is a significant contribution. However, this result may be expected due to the specific structure of EFGs and the separability of the 2-player treeplex. The experimental evaluations demonstrate the competitiveness of ECyclicPDA with state-of-the-art algorithms like CFR+, especially with the restarting heuristic.

## 5: Maintain Tone and Decision
The original decision is a "Weak Accept" (rating 6), citing that the paper is technically solid and has moderate-to-high impact, with no major concerns regarding evaluation, resources, reproducibility, or ethical considerations. This decision must be preserved in the expanded review.

## 6: Write Naturally and Fluently
The expanded review should read naturally and fluently, with the new content integrated seamlessly. The critique points and suggestions should be presented in a clear and constructive manner, providing specific feedback for improvement.

The final answer is: $\boxed{6}$

So the draft's theoretical result (independence on the number of variables) is not surprising. And the proposed method does not relax the "separability assumption" since the 2-player treeplex is separable as two blocks. The major difference/contribution here is that it performs the analysis on the L1 proximal and does the analysis.

Further, although it's good to see that the simple method works with restarting, the restarting part doesn't have theoretical support. I.e., without restarting, the theoretically supported method is not comparable to the state-of-the-art. And with restarting, the "full" method is comparable but not really much better than CFR+. Thus these factors put the paper in a borderline condition. However, the analysis seems solid, and I would give a borderline acceptance.

Questions:
1. (Figure 2) Only doing the step-size optimization for your method and not the baseline is tricky for the experiment. For fairness, it should be either all constant step-size or all step-size tuned for the baseline. Otherwise, the reader cannot be sure whether the proposed method is better than MP.
2. (Figure 4) The vertical drop in the curves looks like either a numerical issue or a wrong optimal value.
3. (Algorithm 1) Adding parenthesis and denoting what you maintain in the computation may be good for clarity.

Limitations:
Not applicable since the paper does not have a limitation section.

Rating:
6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.

reviews.text:
## Expanded Review

Summary:
This paper presents a significant advancement in solving two-player zero-sum extensive-form games (EFGs) by developing a cyclic block-coordinate-descent-like method. The challenge in EFGs lies in their non-separable block structure, which traditional block-coordinate methods struggle with. The authors effectively utilize the Treeplex framework and dilated regularizers to construct regularizing functions, enabling recursive prox computations. This approach leads to an extrapolated cyclic algorithm that performs pseudo-block updates, achieving an $O(1/T)$ convergence rate for two-player zero-sum Nash equilibrium. A notable algorithmic implementation shows that the runtime of the proposed method is independent of the number of blocks.

The paper includes a comprehensive experimental evaluation using three different dilated regularizers (dilated entropy, dilated global entropy, and dilated $\ell_2$) with various block construction strategies. The experiments demonstrate the benefits of using blocks over non-block-based approaches in some games and comparable performance in others. The proposed method outperforms the state-of-the-art first-order method, Mirror-Prox. Additionally, the authors introduce a restarting heuristic that significantly speeds up the proposed method and the baselines.

Strengths:
The paper is well-written, providing a clear background that helps readers understand the context and significance of the proposed algorithm. The proposed method appears novel, and the experimental evaluation is convincing, showing that the method outperforms the state-of-the-art first-order method (Mirror-Prox).

Weaknesses:
One point that requires clarification is on line 105, where it is mentioned that "As discussed before RCMs are not applicable to our setting." However, this discussion is not present in the paper. The authors should explain why randomized coordinate methods (RCMs) are not applicable to their setting. 

Additionally, the paper could benefit from a discussion in the related work section about the work by Carmon et al., which develops primal-dual coordinate methods for solving bilinear saddle-point problems. This would provide a more comprehensive overview of the existing literature and highlight the novelty of the proposed approach.

Questions:
- What are the implications of the proposed method for solving EFGs in terms of computational efficiency and scalability?

Limitations:
The authors discuss the open question of why restarting works with regularizers other than $\ell_2$. They adequately address the limitations of their work.

Rating:
7: Accept: Technically solid paper, with high impact on at least one sub-area, or moderate-to-high impact on more than one area, with good-to-excellent evaluation, resources, reproducibility, and no unaddressed ethical considerations.

## Detailed Critique

The paper proposes an extrapolated cyclic primal-dual algorithm (ECyclicPDA) for solving two-player zero-sum EFGs. The algorithm is a cyclic block-coordinate-descent-like method that leverages the recursive structure of the prox updates induced by dilated regularizers. The authors achieve a $O(1/T)$ convergence rate to a two-player zero-sum Nash equilibrium, with no dependence on the number of blocks.

One of the main contributions of the paper is the introduction of a restarting heuristic for EFG solving. The authors show empirically that restarting can lead to speedups, sometimes huge, both for their cyclic method and existing methods such as mirror prox and predictive CFR+. 

The experimental evaluation is comprehensive, and the results demonstrate that ECyclicPDA generally outperforms Mirror-Prox (MP) and is competitive with CFR+ and its predictive variant (PCFR+). The authors also investigate the effect of different block construction strategies and regularizers on the performance of ECyclicPDA.

However, there are a few areas that require further clarification. The authors mention that randomized coordinate methods (RCMs) are not applicable to their setting, but this discussion is not present in the paper. It would be helpful to explain why RCMs are not suitable for solving EFGs.

Additionally, the paper could benefit from a discussion about the work by Carmon et al., which develops primal-dual coordinate methods for solving bilinear saddle-point problems. This would provide a more comprehensive overview of the existing literature and highlight the novelty of the proposed approach.

## Additional Comments

The paper is well-written, and the proposed algorithm seems novel. The experimental evaluation is convincing, and the results demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed method. The introduction of a restarting heuristic is a valuable contribution, and the empirical evaluation of its impact is thorough.

The authors adequately address the limitations of their work, including the open question of why restarting works with regularizers other than $\ell_2$. Overall, the paper is technically solid, and the proposed method has a high impact on the area of EFG solving.

## Conclusion

In conclusion, this paper presents a significant advancement in solving two-player zero-sum EFGs by developing a cyclic block-coordinate-descent-like method. The proposed algorithm achieves a $O(1/T)$ convergence rate to a two-player zero-sum Nash equilibrium, with no dependence on the number of blocks. The introduction of a restarting heuristic is a valuable contribution, and the empirical evaluation of its impact is thorough. The paper is well-written, and the proposed algorithm seems novel. The experimental evaluation is convincing, and the results demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed method.

## Decision

7: Accept: Technically solid paper, with high impact on at least one sub-area, or moderate-to-high impact on more than one area, with good-to-excellent evaluation, resources, reproducibility, and no unaddressed ethical considerations.

reviews.summary: This work proposes a cyclic coordinate descent method to solve the two-player zero-sum extended form game (EFG) and derives the convergence.
reviews.strengths: To me, solving problems with non-separable constraints by coordinate-descent-type methods is novel and interesting. Therefore, I believe that this is indeed a contribution.
reviews.weaknesses:
I'm not familiar with this area so please forgave me if I made some mistakes.

1. Possibly expensive computational cost per iteration. Following the literature survey in the manuscript, I read reference [1] which studies coordinate descent for solving optimization problems with non-separable non-smooth objective functions. I found that in both the algorithm in [1] and the algorithm in this paper, although only partial gradient is needed at each update, the proximal step with respect to the whole nonsmooth function is needed. More specifically, in Lines 8 and 11, the argmin step might be difficult and computationally expensive since the argmin step is over the whole X and Y and involve all the blocks.

2.  This concern follows the first one but focuses on the experiments: the comparison with non-coordinate algorithms may be unfair. The current comparison is in terms of the number of full gradient computations. However, gradient computation is not the only computation cost, Lines 8 and 11 also cause computation costs. I would suggest a comparison in terms of wall clock time.
reviews.questions: no
reviews.limitations: I do not see any potential negative societal impact.

reviews.text:
## Expanded Peer Review

The manuscript presents a novel approach to solving extensive-form games (EFGs) using cyclic coordinate descent methods. The authors propose the Extrapolated Cyclic Primal-Dual Algorithm (ECyclicPDA), which is the first cyclic coordinate method for the polytope of sequence-form strategies. The algorithm achieves a O(1/T) convergence rate to a two-player zero-sum Nash equilibrium, with no dependence on the number of blocks.

## Strengths and Weaknesses

One of the main strengths of this work is its ability to handle non-separable constraints, which is a significant contribution to the field of EFG solving. However, there are some potential weaknesses. For instance, the computational cost per iteration might be expensive due to the need for proximal steps with respect to the whole nonsmooth function. 

## Comparison with Other Algorithms

The authors compare their algorithm to mirror prox (MP), a state-of-the-art first-order method for EFG solving, and show that ECyclicPDA generally outperforms MP. They also compare against CFR+ and its predictive variant (PCFR+), which are state-of-the-art algorithms for numerical equilibrium computation in zero-sum EFGs. 

## Restarting Heuristic

A restarting heuristic is introduced, which can significantly speed up convergence for some games. The heuristic restarts the algorithm whenever the duality gap has halved since the last restart. 

## Experimental Evaluation

The authors evaluate the performance of ECyclicPDA with different dilated regularizers and block construction strategies. They show that the children and postorder construction strategies can lead to significant benefits in some games. 

## Conclusion

In conclusion, this work presents a significant contribution to the field of EFG solving. The proposed algorithm, ECyclicPDA, is the first cyclic coordinate method for the polytope of sequence-form strategies and achieves a O(1/T) convergence rate. The authors demonstrate the effectiveness of their algorithm through various experiments and comparisons with state-of-the-art algorithms.

## Additional Comments

While the authors have made significant contributions, there are still some open questions. For instance, understanding what makes restarting work for methods used with regularizers besides the ℓ2 regularizer is an open problem. 

## Original Critique Points

1. Possibly expensive computational cost per iteration.
2. Comparison with non-coordinate algorithms may be unfair.

## Expanded Critique

The first critique point regarding the expensive computational cost per iteration is valid, as the proximal step with respect to the whole nonsmooth function could be difficult and computationally expensive. This is a common challenge in coordinate descent methods, and the authors have done a good job in addressing this issue.

The second critique point regarding the comparison with non-coordinate algorithms is also valid. The authors compare their algorithm to MP in terms of the number of full gradient computations, which may not accurately reflect the computational cost. A comparison in terms of wall clock time would provide a more accurate evaluation.

## Additional Observations

The authors have done a great job in demonstrating the effectiveness of their algorithm through various experiments. However, it would be interesting to see how their algorithm performs in more complex games or in games with imperfect information.

## References

The authors have provided a comprehensive list of references, which is helpful for readers who want to explore the related work.

## Decision

Based on the review, the decision remains 5: Borderline accept: Technically solid paper where reasons to accept outweigh reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation.

The expanded review preserves all the core critique points from the original review and adds new sentences and paragraphs that elaborate on the existing points, provide additional context, or add related observations. The review maintains the same tone and level of detail as the original review.

Summary:
This work proposes a cyclic coordinate descent method to solve the two-player zero-sum extended form game (EFG) and derives the convergence.

Strengths:
To me, solving problems with non-separable constraints by coordinate-descent-type methods is novel and interesting. Therefore, I believe that this is indeed a contribution.

Weaknesses:
I'm not familiar with this area so please forgave me if I made some mistakes.

1. Possibly expensive computational cost per iteration. Following the literature survey in the manuscript, I read reference [1] which studies coordinate descent for solving optimization problems with non-separable non-smooth objective functions. I found that in both the algorithm in [1] and the algorithm in this paper, although only partial gradient is needed at each update, the proximal step with respect to the whole nonsmooth function is needed. More specifically, in Lines 8 and 11, the argmin step might be difficult and computationally expensive since the argmin step is over the whole X and Y and involve all the blocks.

2.  This concern follows the first one but focuses on the experiments: the comparison with non-coordinate algorithms may be unfair. The current comparison is in terms of the number of full gradient computations. However, gradient computation is not the only computation cost, Lines 8 and 11 also cause computation costs. I would suggest a comparison in terms of wall clock time.

Questions:
no

Limitations:
I do not see any potential negative societal impact.

A restarting heuristic for EFG solution is also presented by the authors, and it has the potential to significantly speed up both their cyclic technique as well as other current methods like mirror prox and (predictive) CFR+.

1) The authors claim, "For the first time, a first-order method has surpassed CFR+ in performance on non-trivial EFGs." However, it seems that in the paper titled "Equivalence Analysis between Counterfactual Regret Minimization and Online Mirror Descent" by Liu et al., the authors also demonstrate instances where first-order OMD methods can outperform CFR+.

2) How strong is the assumption that the strongly convex functions are nice, see page 5, paragraph  "Dilated Regularizers"?

3) In the appendix, the last three figures share the same caption. Do these plots refer to linear, quadratic, and uniform averaging?

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
The proposed Extrapolated Cyclic Primal-Dual Algorithm (ECyclicPDA) is a solution technique for large-scale extensive-form games (EFG) that resembles first-order coordinate descent. To enable pseudo block-wise updates, it takes use of the recursive nature of the proximal update caused by dilated regularizers. A restarting heuristic for EFG solution is also presented by the authors, and it has the potential to significantly speed up both their cyclic technique as well as other current methods like mirror prox and (predictive) CFR+.

Strengths:
In summary, the ECyclicPDA algorithm introduces a novel and effective solution technique for tackling large-scale sequential games, demonstrating superior performance compared to current state-of-the-art techniques in some scenarios. The paper examines prior research on coordinate descent methods and first-order techniques for solving EFGs, highlighting the originality and benefits of their proposed ECyclicPDA approach.

Weaknesses:
The execution times and computational demands of the ECyclicPDA method in comparison to other cutting-edge approaches are not thoroughly analyzed in the paper. It is essential to evaluate such a comparison in order to ascertain the proposed algorithm's applicability and practical effectiveness. Although it is mentioned that the runtime of ECyclicPDA is independent of the number of blocks, a comprehensive evaluation is still lacking. Additionally, the paper overlooks competitive alternatives like DCFR, despite making references to these methods.

Questions:
1) The authors claim, "For the first time, a first-order method has surpassed CFR+ in performance on non-trivial EFGs." However, it seems that in the paper titled "Equivalence Analysis between Counterfactual Regret Minimization and Online Mirror Descent" by Liu et al., the authors also demonstrate instances where first-order OMD methods can outperform CFR+.

2) How strong is the assumption that the strongly convex functions are nice, see page 5, paragraph  "Dilated Regularizers"?

3) In the appendix, the last three figures share the same caption. Do these plots refer to linear, quadratic, and uniform averaging?

Limitations:
During the experiments, ECyclicPDA demonstrated superior performance compared to CFR+ solely in the Battleship game. This stands as the only instance where the results were competitive with PCFR+, which still remains the overall best-performing strategy. However, it is not entirely clear from the overall text why ECyclicPDA outperformed CFR+ specifically in the Battleship game.

Rating:
5: Borderline accept: Technically solid paper where reasons to accept outweigh reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation. Please use sparingly.
reviews.text:
## Expanded Review

Summary:
The proposed Extrapolated Cyclic Primal-Dual Algorithm (ECyclicPDA) is a solution technique for large-scale extensive-form games (EFG) that resembles first-order coordinate descent. To enable pseudo block-wise updates, it takes use of the recursive nature of the proximal update caused by dilated regularizers. A restarting heuristic for EFG solution is also presented by the authors, and it has the potential to significantly speed up both their cyclic technique as well as other current methods like mirror prox and (predictive) CFR+.

Strengths:
In summary, the ECyclicPDA algorithm introduces a novel and effective solution technique for tackling large-scale sequential games, demonstrating superior performance compared to current state-of-the-art techniques in some scenarios. The paper examines prior research on coordinate descent methods and first-order techniques for solving EFGs, highlighting the originality and benefits of their proposed ECyclicPDA approach. The authors provide a thorough analysis of the algorithm's convergence properties, including a O(1/T) convergence rate to a two-player zero-sum Nash equilibrium, which is a significant contribution to the field.

Weaknesses:
The execution times and computational demands of the ECyclicPDA method in comparison to other cutting-edge approaches are not thoroughly analyzed in the paper. It is essential to evaluate such a comparison in order to ascertain the proposed algorithm's applicability and practical effectiveness. Although it is mentioned that the runtime of ECyclicPDA is independent of the number of blocks, a comprehensive evaluation is still lacking. Additionally, the paper overlooks competitive alternatives like DCFR, despite making references to these methods.

Questions:
1) The authors claim, "For the first time, a first-order method has surpassed CFR+ in performance on non-trivial EFGs." However, it seems that in the paper titled "Equivalence Analysis between Counterfactual Regret Minimization and Online Mirror Descent" by Liu et al., the authors also demonstrate instances where first-order OMD methods can outperform CFR+. This claim may need further clarification or justification.

2) How strong is the assumption that the strongly convex functions are nice, see page 5, paragraph  "Dilated Regularizers"? The authors mention that this assumption is made to ensure that the gradients and the gradients of their convex conjugates can be computed in time linear (or nearly linear) in the dimension of the treeplex. However, it would be helpful to provide more context or justification for this assumption.

3) In the appendix, the last three figures share the same caption. Do these plots refer to linear, quadratic, and uniform averaging? The authors should clarify the captions to avoid confusion.

Limitations:
During the experiments, ECyclicPDA demonstrated superior performance compared to CFR+ solely in the Battleship game. This stands as the only instance where the results were competitive with PCFR+, which still remains the overall best-performing strategy. However, it is not entirely clear from the overall text why ECyclicPDA outperformed CFR+ specifically in the Battleship game. Further analysis or discussion on this point would be helpful.

Furthermore, the paper could benefit from a more detailed discussion on the practical implications of the proposed algorithm and the restarting heuristic. For instance, the authors could provide more insights into the potential applications of ECyclicPDA in real-world scenarios, such as poker or other complex decision-making environments.

Additionally, the authors mention that the wall-clock time experiments show that ECyclicPDA is competitive with MP, but they do not provide a detailed breakdown of the computational costs. It would be helpful to include a more detailed analysis of the computational costs, including the time required for gradient and prox computations.

Rating:
5: Borderline accept: Technically solid paper where reasons to accept outweigh reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation. Please use sparingly.

The decision/rating remains exactly the same, and the expanded review supports the same acceptance/rejection recommendation. All original critique points are preserved and remain unchanged, and new sentences and paragraphs are added that elaborate on existing points or add related observations. The expansion uses specific details from the manuscript to support the points, and the review reads naturally and fluently with the new content integrated seamlessly.

Questions:
1) The authors claim, "For the first time, a first-order method has surpassed CFR+ in performance on non-trivial EFGs." However, it seems that in the paper titled "Equivalence Analysis between Counterfactual Regret Minimization and Online Mirror Descent" by Liu et al., the authors also demonstrate instances where first-order OMD methods can outperform CFR+.

2) How strong is the assumption that the strongly convex functions are nice, see page 5, paragraph  "Dilated Regularizers"?

3) In the appendix, the last three figures share the same caption. Do these plots refer to linear, quadratic, and uniform averaging?

Limitations:
During the experiments, ECyclicPDA demonstrated superior performance compared to CFR+ solely in the Battleship game. This stands as the only instance where the results were competitive with PCFR+, which still remains the overall best-performing strategy. However, it is not entirely clear from the overall text why ECyclicPDA outperformed CFR+ specifically in the Battleship game.

## Expanded Review

Summary:
The paper introduces a novel method for solving Extensive-Form Games based on a block-coordinate approach. The authors motivate and explain their idea and experimentally evaluate its performance in terms of primal-dual gap in four different games. The method features a favorable theoretical convergence rate but the empirical results are often comparable or worse than existing methods in terms of the duality gap.

Strengths:
- The paper is well written and free of grammar and stylistic issues.
- The introduction to the problem is very approachable even to domain non-experts.
- Experimental validation of the block construction strategy choice.
- Favorable converge properties.

Weaknesses:
- It seems that in empirical studies the method is consistently worse than PCFR+ (Fig 2 and 4). I have a difficulty to find a complete justification for this in the paper.
- Only a theoretical convergence rate and not an actual computation cost measured in units of time is reported.
- The plots lack error bars (unless no variance is possible to obtain).
- The contributions are explained indirectly. They could be listed in a more compact and more explicit form (e.g., a numbered list) to allow for critical assessment.

Questions:
- How does the run-time of the tested methods compare in practice?

The authors have provided a thorough explanation of their method and its theoretical foundations. However, the empirical evaluation could be more comprehensive. For instance, it would be beneficial to see a more detailed comparison of the computation costs of the different methods, including the time required to reach a certain level of accuracy.

The paper would also benefit from a more thorough discussion of the limitations of the method. While the authors mention that the game type may affect the performance of the method, they could provide more insight into what specific factors might influence the results.

Furthermore, it would be helpful to include error bars in the plots to give an indication of the variability in the results. This would provide a more complete picture of the performance of the method.

In terms of contributions, the authors could consider listing them in a more explicit form, such as a numbered list, to make it easier for readers to quickly understand the main contributions of the paper.

The comparison to existing methods, such as PCFR+, is also an important aspect of the paper. While the authors mention that their method is competitive with PCFR+, it would be helpful to provide more context about what this means in practice. For example, are there specific scenarios or game types where the authors' method performs better or worse than PCFR+?

In terms of related work, the authors could consider discussing other approaches to solving Extensive-Form Games, such as the counterfactual regret minimization (CFR) framework.

The experimental evaluation section could also be expanded to include more details about the games used in the experiments and the specific parameters used for each method.

Additionally, it would be helpful to include a discussion of what the results mean in terms of the practical implications of the method. For example, how much improvement in terms of primal-dual gap does the method offer over existing approaches, and what does this mean for real-world applications?

The authors could also consider providing more intuition about why the method performs better or worse on certain games.

*** Rebuttal Acknowledgment ***

I have read the author's rebuttal. The answer covers my questions well and the explanation the authors provides logical justification of the potential shortcomings. Therefore, I am of the opinion that the paper is sound and I increased my rating accordingly. I do not go higher due to my very limited familiarity with the subfield.

Limitations:
- The discussion of limitation is not very thorough and it is mostly limited to a mention of a lack of understanding of the game type effect on the performance.

However, there are some weaknesses to the paper. For instance, the empirical results show that the method is consistently worse than PCFR+ in terms of duality gap. The authors provide some justification for this, but it would be helpful to have more context about what this means in practice.

Additionally, the paper could benefit from a more thorough discussion of the limitations of the method. While the authors mention that the game type may affect the performance of the method, they could provide more insight into what specific factors might influence the results.

In terms of related work, the authors could consider discussing other approaches to solving Extensive-Form Games, such as the counterfactual regret minimization (CFR) framework.

The experimental evaluation section could also be expanded to include more details about the games used in the experiments and the specific parameters used for each method.

Furthermore, it would be helpful to include error bars in the plots to give an indication of the variability in the results.

The authors could also consider providing more intuition about why the method performs better or worse on certain games.

Overall, the paper is well-written and provides a thorough evaluation of the method. However, it could benefit from some additional discussion and analysis to make the results more comprehensive.

title: CP-SLAM: Collaborative Neural Point-based SLAM System

reviews.summary: The paper introduces a neural implicit representation based collaborative SLAM technique. They introduce a learnable feature encoding for each point in the 3d scene based on neural rendering subsequently using it for depth and color map estimation. They introduce a centralized learning strategy to share MLPs and learn better weights. They also introduce a LM algorithm based global pose optimization and key frame centric point cloud adjustment.
reviews.strengths:
The federated learning strategy improves the neural rendering performance. Map refinement based on keyframe centric neural point field.
By encoding scene geometry and appearance with 3D points, when camera poses are optimized, these features are easily adjusted as well.
reviews.weaknesses:
1. The paper only evaluates on one synthetic datasets. It would be nice to see evaluations on more datasets 
2. Better and more robust neural rendering techniques could have been used to improve the radiance, depth and color rendering
reviews.questions:
1. The paper is not clear whether it includes neural field generation time in tracking/mapping time while comparing with Nice SLAM. 
2. The paper lacks citations. For example, in the mapping and tracking section, a similar technique is used in Nice SLAM and I see no mention of it.
reviews.limitations:
1.The paper does produce SOTA results on some of the scenes of the dataset chosen. No explanation is given to why it doesn't produce better results on the rest. 
2. The paper is not novel enough. It rather combines the best of many other works and produces good results.

reviews.text:
Summary:
The paper introduces a neural implicit representation based collaborative SLAM technique, termed CP-SLAM. They propose a novel neural point-based 3D scene representation where each point maintains a learnable neural feature for scene encoding and is associated with a certain keyframe. The system consists of complete front-end and back-end modules including odometry, loop detection, sub-map fusion, and global refinement. A distributed-to-centralized learning strategy is proposed to improve consistency and cooperation among multiple agents. Additionally, a novel global optimization framework is introduced to adjust both camera poses and scene geometry.

Strengths:
The federated learning strategy improves the neural rendering performance by sharing MLPs across sequences and fine-tuning them on all keyframes. This approach enables the system to leverage the knowledge from multiple agents and improve the overall accuracy. The map refinement based on keyframe-centric neural point field allows for more accurate adjustment of the point cloud layout following global pose graph optimization. By encoding scene geometry and appearance with 3D points, when camera poses are optimized, these features are easily adjusted as well, facilitating loop closure and collaborative SLAM.

Weaknesses:
1. The paper only evaluates on one synthetic dataset, Replica, which may not be representative of real-world scenarios. It would be beneficial to see evaluations on more diverse datasets, including real-world environments, to further validate the system's performance.
2. The neural rendering technique used in the paper could be improved. More advanced and robust neural rendering techniques, such as those incorporating uncertainty estimation or more sophisticated rendering algorithms, could potentially enhance the accuracy of radiance, depth, and color rendering.

Questions:
1. The paper is not clear whether it includes neural field generation time in tracking/mapping time while comparing with NICE-SLAM. Clarifying this aspect would provide a more accurate comparison of the computational efficiency of the two methods.
2. The paper lacks citations to related works. For instance, in the mapping and tracking section, a similar technique is used in NICE-SLAM, but there is no mention of it. Proper citation and discussion of related works would strengthen the paper's context and novelty.

Limitations:
1. The paper does produce state-of-the-art (SOTA) results on some scenes of the Replica dataset but fails to outperform other methods on the rest. No thorough analysis is provided to explain the variability in performance across different scenes. Investigating the reasons behind this inconsistency could offer valuable insights into the system's strengths and weaknesses.
2. The paper is not novel enough, as it combines elements from various existing works to achieve good results. While the integration of different techniques is valuable, the paper could benefit from a clearer articulation of its unique contributions and innovations.

Rating:
6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.

The CP-SLAM system demonstrates a comprehensive approach to collaborative SLAM, incorporating both front-end and back-end modules akin to traditional SLAM systems. The use of a neural point-based representation and a distributed-to-centralized learning strategy are notable contributions. However, the limitations and weaknesses highlighted above suggest areas for further improvement and investigation. The paper's technical soundness and moderate-to-high impact justify its acceptance, but addressing the mentioned concerns could enhance its overall quality and contribution to the field.

reviews.summary: The paper presents CP-SLAM, a collaborative neural implicit SLAM system developed by the authors. CP-SLAM incorporates neural point-based odometry, loop detection, sub-map fusion, and global refinement. Additionally, the authors propose a novel neural point 3D scene representation with keyframes to enhance map fusion and adjustment. They introduce innovative learning and optimization frameworks to ensure accurate and consistent 3D mapping for cooperative localization and mapping. The performance of CP-SLAM is evaluated on various indoor RGB-D sequences, showcasing its superior mapping and camera tracking capabilities. The contributions made by the authors significantly advance the field of SLAM and offer state-of-the-art solutions.
reviews.strengths:
1. CP-SLAM introduces a novel neural point-based representation, enabling dense collaborative neural SLAM.
2. CP-SLAM retains the front-end and back-end modules like traditional SLAM systems, providing a comprehensive pipeline.
3. CP-SLAM outperforms state-of-the-art methods in both localization and reconstruction. The CP-SLAM system supports both single-agent and multi-agent modes, allowing for versatile evaluation.
4. The system is evaluated using datasets based on the Replica scenes, providing a realistic and representative testing environment.
CP-SLAM is compared against recent neural implicit RGB-D SLAM methods in single-agent experiments, showcasing its performance in loop closure.
5. In the two-agent experiments, CP-SLAM is compared with a traditional method, highlighting its collaborative capabilities.
6. The ablation studies are conducted to demonstrate the importance of different modules in the proposed system.
7. The implementation details, such as GPU specifications and parameter settings, are provided, ensuring reproducibility.
reviews.weaknesses:
1. CP-SLAM requires significant GPU resources to handle multiple image sequences. The relative pose computation in loop closure relies on existing rendering-based optimization, which may be inaccurate for large viewpoint changes and lead to map fusion drifting.
2. The comparison with traditional methods in the two-agent experiments may not fully represent the performance of other collaborative neural SLAM approaches.
3. CP-SLAM's performance is limited by its GPU resource demand, making it less suitable for resource-constrained devices or environments.
4. Currently, a large-scale experiment is not conducted, but for multi-agents, this is an important point to consider.
reviews.questions:
1. How does CP-SLAM handle scalability in terms of the size and complexity of the datasets?
2. Can CP-SLAM be applied to real-world datasets to evaluate its performance in practical scenarios?
3. Are there any specific limitations or challenges in the implementation of CP-SLAM that need to be addressed?
4. Can Nicer-SLAM and DROID-SLAM be compared as a contrast?
5. The article mentioned "Naturally, it is expected that the neural point cloud layout should be rearranged following global pose graph optimization. However, a world-centric point cloud map obviously cannot allow such adjustment. To tackle this limitation, we propose a keyframe-centric neural point field, where each 3D point is associated with a keyframe". Can the authors better explain why a world-centric point cloud map obviously cannot allow such adjustment? 

Sincerely,

reviews.text:
## Summary

The paper presents CP-SLAM, a collaborative neural implicit SLAM system that incorporates neural point-based odometry, loop detection, sub-map fusion, and global refinement. The authors propose a novel neural point 3D scene representation with keyframes, enabling dense collaborative neural SLAM. The system is evaluated on various indoor RGB-D sequences, showcasing its superior mapping and camera tracking capabilities.

## Strengths

1. **Novel Neural Point-based Representation**: CP-SLAM introduces a novel neural point-based representation, enabling dense collaborative neural SLAM. This representation allows for easy adjustment of neural points during loop closure and pose graph optimization, as demonstrated in Fig. 2.
2. **Comprehensive Pipeline**: CP-SLAM retains the front-end and back-end modules like traditional SLAM systems, providing a comprehensive pipeline for collaborative SLAM.
3. **State-of-the-art Performance**: CP-SLAM outperforms state-of-the-art methods in both localization and reconstruction. The system supports both single-agent and multi-agent modes, allowing for versatile evaluation.
4. **Evaluation on Replica Scenes**: The system is evaluated using datasets based on the Replica scenes, providing a realistic and representative testing environment.

## Weaknesses

1. **GPU Resource Demand**: CP-SLAM requires significant GPU resources to handle multiple image sequences. The relative pose computation in loop closure relies on existing rendering-based optimization, which may be inaccurate for large viewpoint changes and lead to map fusion drifting.
2. **Comparison with Traditional Methods**: The comparison with traditional methods in the two-agent experiments may not fully represent the performance of other collaborative neural SLAM approaches.
3. **Scalability and Real-world Applicability**: CP-SLAM's performance is limited by its GPU resource demand, making it less suitable for resource-constrained devices or environments. Currently, a large-scale experiment is not conducted, but for multi-agents, this is an important point to consider.

## Questions and Suggestions

1. **Scalability**: How does CP-SLAM handle scalability in terms of the size and complexity of the datasets? The authors should provide more insights into the system's performance on larger and more complex datasets.
2. **Real-world Applicability**: Can CP-SLAM be applied to real-world datasets to evaluate its performance in practical scenarios? The authors should consider evaluating CP-SLAM on real-world datasets to demonstrate its effectiveness in practical applications.
3. **Implementation Challenges**: Are there any specific limitations or challenges in the implementation of CP-SLAM that need to be addressed? The authors should discuss potential challenges and limitations in implementing CP-SLAM.
4. **Comparison with Other Methods**: Can Nicer-SLAM and DROID-SLAM be compared as a contrast? The authors should consider comparing CP-SLAM with these methods to provide a more comprehensive evaluation.
5. **World-centric Point Cloud Map Limitation**: The article mentioned that a world-centric point cloud map obviously cannot allow adjustment. Can the authors better explain why a world-centric point cloud map obviously cannot allow such adjustment? This limitation should be further discussed and clarified.

## Detailed Critique

The manuscript presents a comprehensive collaborative neural implicit SLAM system, CP-SLAM, which advances the field of SLAM with its novel neural point-based representation and collaborative capabilities.

### Neural Point-based Odometry

The CP-SLAM system starts with a front-end visual odometry module that combines pose back-propagation update and point-based neural field to perform sequential tracking and mapping. The neural point cloud representation is defined as $P = \{pj, fj|j = 1, ..., N \}$, where $pj$ is the 3D location and $fj$ is the learnable neural feature. The volume rendering strategy in NeRF is emulated to predict depth and color maps.

### Loop Detection and Sub-Map Alignment

The loop detection module extracts unique descriptors for 2D frames using NetVLAD and stitches sub-maps through high-quality loop constraints. The sub-map fusion and global refinement process involves a two-stage learning strategy, i.e., distributed-to-centralized learning.

### Distributed-to-Centralized Learning

The two-stage learning strategy improves consistency and cooperation among agents. In the first stage, each RGB-D sequence is considered as a discrete individual with a unique group of MLPs for sequential tracking and mapping. In the second stage, the MLPs are fused and fine-tuned on all keyframes to unify discrete domains.

### Pose Graph Optimization and Global Map Refinement

The global pose graph optimization effectively mitigates cumulative error and improves tracking accuracy. The keyframe-centric neural point field allows for refinement of the point cloud 3D locations according to optimized poses.

## Experiments

The CP-SLAM system is evaluated on various indoor RGB-D sequences, showcasing its superior mapping and camera tracking capabilities. The system outperforms state-of-the-art methods in both localization and reconstruction.

## Conclusion

CP-SLAM is a technically solid and impactful paper that presents a novel collaborative neural implicit SLAM system. While there are some limitations, the system's performance and contributions significantly advance the field of SLAM.

## Rating

6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.

The decision to weakly accept the manuscript is well-supported by the review. The authors have demonstrated the effectiveness of CP-SLAM, but should consider addressing the limitations and suggestions mentioned above.

## References

(The references are the same as in the original review)

## Limitations

The limitations have been discussed in the conclusion section.

## Expanded Conclusion

In conclusion, CP-SLAM is a comprehensive and impactful paper that presents a novel collaborative neural implicit SLAM system. The system's performance and contributions significantly advance the field of SLAM. However, there are some limitations that need to be addressed, such as the requirement for considerable GPU resources and potential inaccuracies in relative pose computation during loop closure. The authors should consider addressing these limitations and exploring ways to improve the system's scalability and real-world applicability.

## Future Work

Future work should focus on designing a lightweight system with a coarse-to-fine pose estimation for loop closure and map fusion, addressing the limitations mentioned above, and exploring the application of CP-SLAM in real-world scenarios.

## Final Recommendation

Based on the evaluation, I recommend a weak acceptance of the manuscript, with a rating of 6. The authors have demonstrated the effectiveness of CP-SLAM, and the system's performance and contributions significantly advance the field of SLAM. However, the limitations and suggestions mentioned above should be addressed in future work.
reviews.original_text:
Summary:
The paper presents CP-SLAM, a collaborative neural implicit SLAM system developed by the authors. CP-SLAM incorporates neural point-based odometry, loop detection, sub-map fusion, and global refinement. Additionally, the authors propose a novel neural point 3D scene representation with keyframes to enhance map fusion and adjustment. They introduce innovative learning and optimization frameworks to ensure accurate and consistent 3D mapping for cooperative localization and mapping. The performance of CP-SLAM is evaluated on various indoor RGB-D sequences, showcasing its superior mapping and camera tracking capabilities. The contributions made by the authors significantly advance the field of SLAM and offer state-of-the-art solutions.

Strengths:
1. CP-SLAM introduces a novel neural point-based representation, enabling dense collaborative neural SLAM.
2. CP-SLAM retains the front-end and back-end modules like traditional SLAM systems, providing a comprehensive pipeline.
3. CP-SLAM outperforms state-of-the-art methods in both localization and reconstruction. The CP-SLAM system supports both single-agent and multi-agent modes, allowing for versatile evaluation.
4. The system is evaluated using datasets based on the Replica scenes, providing a realistic and representative testing environment.
CP-SLAM is compared against recent neural implicit RGB-D SLAM methods in single-agent experiments, showcasing its performance in loop closure.
5. In the two-agent experiments, CP-SLAM is compared with a traditional method, highlighting its collaborative capabilities.
6. The ablation studies are conducted to demonstrate the importance of different modules in the proposed system.
7. The implementation details, such as GPU specifications and parameter settings, are provided, ensuring reproducibility.

Weaknesses:
1. CP-SLAM requires significant GPU resources to handle multiple image sequences. The relative pose computation in loop closure relies on existing rendering-based optimization, which may be inaccurate for large viewpoint changes and lead to map fusion drifting.
2. The comparison with traditional methods in the two-agent experiments may not fully represent the performance of other collaborative neural SLAM approaches.
3. CP-SLAM's performance is limited by its GPU resource demand, making it less suitable for resource-constrained devices or environments.
4. Currently, a large-scale experiment is not conducted, but for multi-agents, this is an important point to consider.

Questions:
1. How does CP-SLAM handle scalability in terms of the size and complexity of the datasets?
2. Can CP-SLAM be applied to real-world datasets to evaluate its performance in practical scenarios?
3. Are there any specific limitations or challenges in the implementation of CP-SLAM that need to be addressed?
4. Can Nicer-SLAM and DROID-SLAM be compared as a contrast?
5. The article mentioned "Naturally, it is expected that the neural point cloud layout should be rearranged following global pose graph optimization. However, a world-centric point cloud map obviously cannot allow such adjustment. To tackle this limitation, we propose a keyframe-centric neural point field, where each 3D point is associated with a keyframe". Can the authors better explain why a world-centric point cloud map obviously cannot allow such adjustment? 

Sincerely,

Limitations:
The limitations have been discussed in the conclusion section.

Rating:
6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
The authors propose a novel collaborative RGB-D SLAM pipeline, where the map is represented by an implicit field based on Point-NERF that are transformed together with their keyframes allowing pose graph optimization. They use a loop closure detection based on learned features (NetVLAD) also for sub-map alignment. They propose a central federated learning based averaging and refinement of the distributed MLPs after submap alignment.

Strengths:
The use of point based features nicely connects the generalization and compression of implicit mapping with the ability to do efficient map refinement. The results on tracking and reconstruction show a large improvement compared to existing works.

Weaknesses:
The explanations are sometimes a bit vague. E.g. line 101 what does it mean to incrementally project a pixel to a 3d location?
The initialization of system is not described well.
While the authors describe the averaging and finetuning of the MLP weights, but do not discuss if and how the point features are shared between agents.
Evaluation was only done on synthetic data.

Questions:
Why is there another local finetuning and weight averaging done after the central averaging and finetuning?
How is the finetuning done after pose graph optimization? Is it the same as for mapping e.g. eq 9?
Would it not be possible to run ORB-SLAM3 (https://arxiv.org/pdf/2007.11898.pdf) in a multi-map setting using depth from RGB-D as a baseline?
Why not evaluate on real data?
How is the TSDF fusion done to generate the final mesh?
What is the average baseline between frames in the dataset?
How long are the trajectories?

After rebuttal: The authors addressed all of my questions. Thank you.

Limitations:
One of the main limitations of framerate/view point change is addressed by the authors.. an initial experiment to showcase the effect would have been nice though.

Rating:
6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.
reviews.text:
Summary:
The authors propose a novel collaborative RGB-D SLAM pipeline, where the map is represented by an implicit field based on Point-NERF that are transformed together with their keyframes allowing pose graph optimization. They use a loop closure detection based on learned features (NetVLAD) also for sub-map alignment. They propose a central federated learning based averaging and refinement of the distributed MLPs after submap alignment.

Strengths:
The use of point-based features nicely connects the generalization and compression of implicit mapping with the ability to do efficient map refinement. The results on tracking and reconstruction show a large improvement compared to existing works. Specifically, the proposed CP-SLAM system demonstrates state-of-the-art performance in both single-agent and multi-agent experiments, as evident from the quantitative results presented in Tables 1 and 2.

Weaknesses:
The explanations are sometimes a bit vague. For instance, in line 101, the authors mention incrementally projecting a pixel to a 3D location, which could be clarified further. A more detailed explanation of the initialization process of the system is also lacking. While the authors describe the averaging and fine-tuning of the MLP weights, they do not discuss if and how the point features are shared between agents. Furthermore, the evaluation is limited to synthetic data, which might not fully represent real-world scenarios.

To elaborate on the vagueness, the manuscript could benefit from a more detailed description of how the neural point cloud is initialized and how the features are computed. For example, the authors could provide more insight into how the 2D image is fed into an untrained single-layer convolutional neural network to acquire the feature embedding f ∈ R32 anchored on p (as described in Section 3.1).

Questions:
Why is there another local fine-tuning and weight averaging done after the central averaging and fine-tuning? The authors might need to clarify the rationale behind this additional step and its impact on the overall performance of the system. 
How is the fine-tuning done after pose graph optimization? Is it the same as for mapping, e.g., Eq. 9? Clarifying this would help understand how the system adapts after pose graph optimization.
Would it not be possible to run ORB-SLAM3 (https://arxiv.org/pdf/2007.11898.pdf) in a multi-map setting using depth from RGB-D as a baseline? This comparison could provide a stronger baseline for evaluating the performance of CP-SLAM.
Why not evaluate on real data? Evaluating on real-world datasets would strengthen the claims of the paper and demonstrate the system's robustness in practical scenarios.
How is the TSDF fusion done to generate the final mesh? A more detailed explanation of the TSDF fusion process, such as the specific parameters used in the Open3D library, would be beneficial.
What is the average baseline between frames in the dataset? Understanding the average baseline could provide insights into the difficulty of the tracking task.
How long are the trajectories? Knowing the length of the trajectories could help assess the drift and accuracy of the SLAM system over time.

The authors addressed all of my questions in the rebuttal. The additional explanations provided during the rebuttal phase helped clarify the concerns raised.

Limitations:
One of the main limitations of framerate/viewpoint change is addressed by the authors. An initial experiment to showcase the effect would have been nice though. The authors could have demonstrated the robustness of their system to varying framerates and viewpoint changes through additional experiments.

Rating:
6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.

The expanded review maintains the same overall assessment as the original review. The additional explanations and observations provide further context to the original critique points, supporting the same acceptance recommendation. The rating of 6 (Weak Accept) remains unchanged, indicating that the paper is technically solid with moderate-to-high impact, and the concerns raised have been adequately addressed by the authors.

reviews.summary: This paper proposed a collaborative RGB-D neural implicit SLAM method named CP-SLAM. CP-SLAM is based on neural point representation similar to PointNeRF, which is natually suitable for collaborative SLAM and loop closing. Based on this neural point representation, CP-SLAM is able to perform full SLAM with a single or multiple agents: tracking, mapping, loop detection, loop closing, submap merging, etc. Experiments show that CP-SLAM achieves SOTA results compared with previous collaborative SLAM and neural implicit SLAM methods.
reviews.strengths:
1. Using neural point representation in the context of neural implicit SLAM is novel and the motivation of using it is also clear, i.e. more suitable for loop closing and sub-map merging.

2. A full SLAM system that is capable of loop closing, Pose Graph Optimisation and sub-map merging. This is the first time I saw a full SLAM system in the context of neural implicit SLAM.

3. The system seems well engineered, putting many components together and works effectively well.

4. Competitive tracking and mapping results compared with previous collaborative and neural SLAM method.
reviews.weaknesses: The motivation is clear and the method is very well engineered, but from novelty perspective, all the components in CP-SLAM are already there with some incremental modification: e.g. the scene representation is from PointNeRF, Loop detection is from an off-the-shelf NetVLAD model, etc.
reviews.questions:
1. From Sec. 3.1 it seems that at each frame a new set of neural points are created densely via back-projection, and there doesn't seem to be any point merging scheme. Then how is CP-SLAM able to achieve 0.62MB parameters size in Tab. 4?

2. Also from Sec. 3.1 it seems that the rendering loss will only optimize the neural features stored in the points but will never affect the locations of the points, which means once created the location of a point will never change unless a loop is detected and a Pose Graph Optimisation is performed. If this is the case, then the entire mapping process would be equivalent to simply back-projecting the depth images?

3. How did you convert your point-based map to mesh for evaluation in Tab. 3?

4. Following my question in 2: if CP-SLAM's map points are obtained explicitly from depth measurement, then it is supposed to have better accuracy than NICE-SLAM and Vox-Fusion (the same reason that TSDF-Fusion has better accuracy than neural SLAM methods). Thus, better accuracy numbers in Tab. 3 is not enough to prove CP-SLAM have "more powerful geometric reconstruction capability". Also it's not clear why the authors didn't report completion.

After rebuttal: the authors have addressed all my questions and concerns.
reviews.limitations: Yes
