reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This paper proposed a collaborative RGB-D neural implicit SLAM method named CP-SLAM. CP-SLAM is based on neural point representation similar to PointNeRF, which is natually suitable for collaborative SLAM and loop closing. Based on this neural point representation, CP-SLAM is able to perform full SLAM with a single or multiple agents: tracking, mapping, loop detection, loop closing, submap merging, etc. Experiments show that CP-SLAM achieves SOTA results compared with previous collaborative SLAM and neural implicit SLAM methods.

Strengths:
1. Using neural point representation in the context of neural implicit SLAM is novel and the motivation of using it is also clear, i.e. more suitable for loop closing and sub-map merging.

2. A full SLAM system that is capable of loop closing, Pose Graph Optimisation and sub-map merging. This is the first time I saw a full SLAM system in the context of neural implicit SLAM.

3. The system seems well engineered, putting many components together and works effectively well.

4. Competitive tracking and mapping results compared with previous collaborative and neural SLAM method.

Weaknesses:
The motivation is clear and the method is very well engineered, but from novelty perspective, all the components in CP-SLAM are already there with some incremental modification: e.g. the scene representation is from PointNeRF, Loop detection is from an off-the-shelf NetVLAD model, etc.

Questions:
1. From Sec. 3.1 it seems that at each frame a new set of neural points are created densely via back-projection, and there doesn't seem to be any point merging scheme. Then how is CP-SLAM able to achieve 0.62MB parameters size in Tab. 4?

2. Also from Sec. 3.1 it seems that the rendering loss will only optimize the neural features stored in the points but will never affect the locations of the points, which means once created the location of a point will never change unless a loop is detected and a Pose Graph Optimisation is performed. If this is the case, then the entire mapping process would be equivalent to simply back-projecting the depth images?

3. How did you convert your point-based map to mesh for evaluation in Tab. 3?

4. Following my question in 2: if CP-SLAM's map points are obtained explicitly from depth measurement, then it is supposed to have better accuracy than NICE-SLAM and Vox-Fusion (the same reason that TSDF-Fusion has better accuracy than neural SLAM methods). Thus, better accuracy numbers in Tab. 3 is not enough to prove CP-SLAM have "more powerful geometric reconstruction capability". Also it's not clear why the authors didn't report completion.

After rebuttal: the authors have addressed all my questions and concerns.

Limitations:
Yes

Rating:
6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.
reviews.text:
Summary:
This paper proposed a collaborative RGB-D neural implicit SLAM method named CP-SLAM, which leverages a novel neural point representation similar to PointNeRF. This representation is well-suited for collaborative SLAM and loop closing. CP-SLAM is capable of performing full SLAM with a single or multiple agents, including tracking, mapping, loop detection, loop closing, submap merging, and more. The experiments demonstrate that CP-SLAM achieves state-of-the-art results compared to previous collaborative SLAM and neural implicit SLAM methods.

Strengths:
1. The use of neural point representation in the context of neural implicit SLAM is novel, and the motivation behind it is clear: it is more suitable for loop closing and sub-map merging.

2. CP-SLAM presents a complete SLAM system capable of loop closing, Pose Graph Optimization, and sub-map merging. This is the first instance of a full SLAM system in the context of neural implicit SLAM.

3. The system appears well-engineered, integrating multiple components effectively.

4. CP-SLAM achieves competitive tracking and mapping results compared to previous collaborative and neural SLAM methods.

Weaknesses:
While the motivation is clear and the method is well-engineered, from a novelty perspective, all components in CP-SLAM are already present with some incremental modifications. For example, the scene representation is from PointNeRF, and loop detection is from an off-the-shelf NetVLAD model.

Questions and Further Observations:
1. In Sec. 3.1, it seems that a new set of neural points is created densely via back-projection at each frame, without any point merging scheme. How does CP-SLAM achieve a parameter size of 0.62MB in Tab. 4? This suggests that there might be an underlying mechanism for managing the number of neural points.

2. From Sec. 3.1, the rendering loss optimizes the neural features stored in the points but does not affect their locations. This implies that once created, the location of a point does not change unless a loop is detected and Pose Graph Optimization is performed. If so, the mapping process would be equivalent to back-projecting the depth images. This could be a potential limitation in terms of flexibility and adaptability to changing environments.

3. The conversion of the point-based map to a mesh for evaluation in Tab. 3 is not detailed. Clarifying this process would help assess the accuracy of the mesh generation.

4. If CP-SLAM's map points are obtained explicitly from depth measurements, it should have better accuracy than NICE-SLAM and Vox-Fusion. The better accuracy numbers in Tab. 3 are not sufficient to prove CP-SLAM has a "more powerful geometric reconstruction capability" without considering other factors like completion.

The authors have addressed all questions and concerns raised.

Limitations:
Yes, there are limitations. One significant limitation is the requirement for considerable GPU resources to operate multiple image sequences. Additionally, CP-SLAM has a slightly weaker hole-filling ability in unobserved regions compared to feature grid-based methods. The relative pose computation in loop closure relies on existing rendering-based optimization, which may be inaccurate for large viewpoint changes, potentially leading to drifting of map fusion.

Rating:
6: Weak Accept - Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns regarding evaluation, resources, reproducibility, or ethical considerations.

The expanded review maintains the same decision and rating as the original review while elaborating on the existing points and adding related observations. It highlights the strengths and weaknesses of CP-SLAM and provides a comprehensive analysis of the method's capabilities and limitations.

reviews.summary: The paper presents a collaborative implicit neural simultaneous localization and mapping (SLAM) system for RGB-D image sequences, which is able to merge sub-maps into a global map and also handle loop-closure for neural mapper for the first time. To facilitate the capability of doing loop-closure and sub-map merging, the authors introduce a neural point-based 3D scene representation, where each point maintains a learnable neural feature for scene encoding and is associated with a keyframe. The optimization follows the traditional SLAM’s graph optimization fashion. The experimental results on diverse datasets demonstrate the superior performance of the proposed method in both camera tracking and mapping.
reviews.strengths:
1. Novel Neural Point-based 3D Scene Representation: A neural point cloud representation for scene encoding in the proposed framework is a significant contribution. This approach allows each point to carry encoded information, improving the system's ability to represent and cross-view association for large-scale tracking and pose refinement. 
2.Comprehensive Collaborative Framework: The authors provide a collaborative SLAM system with front-end and back-end modules, addressing essential aspects such as odometry, loop detection, sub-map fusion, and global refinement. This comprehensive approach ensures that the proposed system can handle various challenges typically encountered in SLAM tasks. 
3.Distributed-to-Centralized Learning Strategy: The incorporation of a distributed-to-centralized learning strategy is an effective solution for improving consistency and cooperation in collaborative implicit SLAM. 
4.Improved Performance: The experimental results on multiple datasets demonstrate the superiority of the proposed method in both camera tracking and mapping.
reviews.weaknesses:
1.In the Introduction: This paper emphasizes the significance of targeting collaborative-SLAM, considering the substantial value already provided by loop-closure capability in effectively reducing pose drift. However, the evaluation of the paper becomes more challenging due to the necessity for additional comparative studies that can convincingly demonstrate the advantages offered by both approaches.

2.In Section 2: For collaborative mapping, this paper should cite more recent works including:
[1*] Campos, Carlos, et al. "Orb-slam3: An accurate open-source library for visual, visual–inertial, and multimap slam." IEEE Transactions on Robotics 37.6 (2021): 1874-1890.
[2*] Tian, Yulun, et al. "Kimera-multi: Robust, distributed, dense metric-semantic slam for multi-robot systems." IEEE Transactions on Robotics 38.4 (2022).

It is also expected to provide comparative studies to the above researches.

3.In Section 3: 
(1). Line 101: This paper introduced 4x4 patches to compute the point features. However, it misses details on there is a limitation on the number of patches. What if we increase the size of the patches? Will it impact the later performance? 
(2) This paper introduced so many symbols but without giving any explanation of the symbols, which makes it really hard to follow the paper. Please revise the whole paper and make them clear to the readers.
(3) Equation 10: The explanation “Considering the strong non-convexity of the color map, only the geometry part is included in tracking loss” for not using color in the loss is not convincing. The authors are expected to provide ablation studies to prove the claim.

4.In Section 4: More experiments are expected:
(1). More comparative studies on Collaborative-SLAM are expected.
(2). For single-agent cases: The local covisibility map can be formed as well; it would be interesting to only look at cases without a loop but having a local covisibility map.
(3). Ablation study on “Distributed-to-Centralized Learning” is needed to prove why this is needed.
reviews.questions:
1.In the context of this paper, which relies on point features for establishing frame-to-frame correspondence, an important concern arises regarding the maintenance of continuous tracking. Unlike the traditional SLAM pipeline, where feature locations typically reside around corners, this paper selects points without necessarily adhering to this characteristic. Thus, it becomes crucial to address how this approach ensures the continuity of tracking throughout the SLAM process.
2.Collaborative mapping can be done by recent work on No Pose Nerf:
[]Bian, Wenjing, et al. "Nope-nerf: Optimising neural radiance field with no pose prior." Proceedings of the IEEE/CVF Conference on Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition. 2023.
This paper should provide a comparison to show the advantage of the proposed method.
3. This paper has too many contributions, which does not form a scientific paper but more an engineering paper. Not sure if this paper fall into the NeuralIPS group.
reviews.limitations:
1. The writing should be improved, especially the equations should be better explained.
2. More experiments and ablation studies should be provided to demonstrate the advantage of the proposed algorithms. 
3. The literature review needs to refer to more recent research.
reviews.rating: 6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.

However, the evaluation of the paper becomes more challenging due to the necessity for additional comparative studies that can convincingly demonstrate the advantages offered by both approaches.

2.In Section 2: For collaborative mapping, this paper should cite more recent works including:
[1*] Campos, Carlos, et al. "Orb-slam3: An accurate open-source library for visual, visual–inertial, and multimap slam." IEEE Transactions on Robotics 37.6 (2021): 1874-1890.
[2*] Tian, Yulun, et al. "Kimera-multi: Robust, distributed, dense metric-semantic slam for multi-robot systems." IEEE Transactions on Robotics 38.4 (2022).

It is also expected to provide comparative studies to the above researches.

3.In Section 3: 
(1). Line 101: This paper introduced 4x4 patches to compute the point features. However, it misses details on there is a limitation on the number of patches. What if we increase the size of the patches? Will it impact the later performance? 
(2) This paper introduced so many symbols but without giving any explanation of the symbols, which makes it really hard to follow the paper. Please revise the whole paper and make them clear to the readers.
(3) Equation 10: The explanation “Considering the strong non-convexity of the color map, only the geometry part is included in tracking loss” for not using color in the loss is not convincing. The authors are expected to provide ablation studies to prove the claim.

4.In Section 4: More experiments are expected:
(1). More comparative studies on Collaborative-SLAM are expected.
(2). For single-agent cases: The local covisibility map can be formed as well; it would be interesting to only look at cases without a loop but having a local covisibility map.
(3). Ablation study on “Distributed-to-Centralized Learning” is needed to prove why this is needed.

Questions:
1.In the context of this paper, which relies on point features for establishing frame-to-frame correspondence, an important concern arises regarding the maintenance of continuous tracking. Unlike the traditional SLAM pipeline, where feature locations typically reside around corners, this paper selects points without necessarily adhering to this characteristic. Thus, it becomes crucial to address how this approach ensures the continuity of tracking throughout the SLAM process.
2.Collaborative mapping can be done by recent work on No Pose Nerf:
[]Bian, Wenjing, et al. "Nope-nerf: Optimising neural radiance field with no pose prior." Proceedings of the IEEE/CVF Conference on Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition. 2023.
This paper should provide a comparison to show the advantage of the proposed method.
3. This paper has too many contributions, which does not form a scientific paper but more an engineering paper. Not sure if this paper fall into the NeuralIPS group.

Limitations:
1. The writing should be improved, especially the equations should be better explained.
2. More experiments and ablation studies should be provided to demonstrate the advantage of the proposed algorithms. 
3. The literature review needs to refer to more recent research.

Rating:
6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.
reviews.text:
Summary:
The paper presents a collaborative implicit neural simultaneous localization and mapping (SLAM) system for RGB-D image sequences, which is able to merge sub-maps into a global map and also handle loop-closure for neural mapper for the first time. To facilitate the capability of doing loop-closure and sub-map merging, the authors introduce a neural point-based 3D scene representation, where each point maintains a learnable neural feature for scene encoding and is associated with a keyframe. The optimization follows the traditional SLAM’s graph optimization fashion. The experimental results on diverse datasets demonstrate the superior performance of the proposed method in both camera tracking and mapping.

Strengths:
1. **Novel Neural Point-based 3D Scene Representation**: A neural point cloud representation for scene encoding in the proposed framework is a significant contribution. This approach allows each point to carry encoded information, improving the system's ability to represent and cross-view association for large-scale tracking and pose refinement. The neural point-based representation enables the system to effectively handle loop-closure and sub-map merging.

2. **Comprehensive Collaborative Framework**: The authors provide a collaborative SLAM system with front-end and back-end modules, addressing essential aspects such as odometry, loop detection, sub-map fusion, and global refinement. This comprehensive approach ensures that the proposed system can handle various challenges typically encountered in SLAM tasks.

3. **Distributed-to-Centralized Learning Strategy**: The incorporation of a distributed-to-centralized learning strategy is an effective solution for improving consistency and cooperation in collaborative implicit SLAM. This strategy enables the system to adapt to changing environments and improve its performance over time.

4. **Improved Performance**: The experimental results on multiple datasets demonstrate the superiority of the proposed method in both camera tracking and mapping. The system shows robust performance in various scenarios, including single-agent and multi-agent settings.

Weaknesses:
1. **Introduction**: This paper emphasizes the significance of targeting collaborative-SLAM, considering the substantial value already provided by loop-closure capability in effectively reducing pose drift. However, the evaluation of the paper becomes more challenging due to the necessity for additional comparative studies that can convincingly demonstrate the advantages offered by both approaches. For instance, a more detailed comparison with recent works, such as ORB-SLAM3 and Kimera-Multi, would provide a clearer understanding of the proposed system's benefits.

2. **Related Work**: The paper could benefit from a more comprehensive review of recent works in collaborative mapping, including:
   - Campos, Carlos, et al. "ORB-SLAM3: An accurate open-source library for visual, visual–inertial, and multimap SLAM." IEEE Transactions on Robotics 37.6 (2021): 1874-1890.
   - Tian, Yulun, et al. "Kimera-Multi: Robust, distributed, dense metric-semantic SLAM for multi-robot systems." IEEE Transactions on Robotics 38.4 (2022).

3. **Methodology**:
   - **Patch Size Limitation**: The paper introduced 4x4 patches to compute point features. However, it misses details on potential limitations on the number of patches. Increasing the size of the patches may impact the later performance, and the authors should discuss this trade-off.
   - **Symbol Explanation**: The paper introduced many symbols without clear explanations, making it hard to follow. Revising the whole paper to make these symbols clear to readers is necessary.

4. **Tracking Loss**:
   - **Color Map Convexity**: The explanation “Considering the strong non-convexity of the color map, only the geometry part is included in tracking loss” for not using color in the loss is not convincing. The authors are expected to provide ablation studies to prove this claim.

5. **Experiments**:
   - **Comparative Studies**: More comparative studies on collaborative-SLAM are expected, particularly with recent methods like NoPe-Nerf.
   - **Single-Agent Cases**: It would be interesting to examine cases without a loop but with a local covisibility map.
   - **Ablation Study**: An ablation study on the “Distributed-to-Centralized Learning” strategy is needed to prove its necessity.

Questions:
1. **Tracking Continuity**: Unlike traditional SLAM pipelines, where feature locations typically reside around corners, this paper selects points without necessarily adhering to this characteristic. The authors should address how this approach ensures the continuity of tracking throughout the SLAM process.

2. **Collaborative Mapping**: A comparison with recent work on NoPe-Nerf, which focuses on collaborative mapping without pose prior, would be beneficial.

3. **Contributions**: The paper has multiple contributions, which might make it seem more like an engineering paper than a scientific one. It is essential to ensure that the paper's contributions are well-organized and clearly justified.

Limitations:
1. **Writing Improvement**: The writing should be improved, especially the explanations of equations.
2. **Additional Experiments**: More experiments and ablation studies should be provided to demonstrate the advantage of the proposed algorithms.
3. **Literature Review**: The literature review needs to refer to more recent research.

Rating:
6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.

**Expanded Decision (Rating)**: 
The expanded review preserves the same rating of 6, indicating a weak accept. The paper is technically solid and has a moderate-to-high impact. However, to strengthen the paper, the authors should address the mentioned weaknesses, provide additional experiments, and improve the clarity of their explanations.

### Expanded Review

The proposed CP-SLAM system presents a significant advancement in collaborative implicit neural SLAM. The introduction of a neural point-based 3D scene representation is a notable contribution, enabling efficient loop-closure and sub-map merging. This representation allows for learnable neural features associated with each point, enhancing scene encoding and cross-view association.

The comprehensive framework of CP-SLAM addresses critical components of SLAM tasks, including odometry, loop detection, sub-map fusion, and global refinement. The distributed-to-centralized learning strategy is a valuable addition, improving consistency and cooperation in collaborative SLAM.

The experimental results demonstrate the system's superior performance in both single-agent and multi-agent settings. However, to further validate the system's capabilities, additional comparative studies with recent methods, such as ORB-SLAM3 and Kimera-Multi, are necessary.

The paper could benefit from a more detailed explanation of the patch size limitation and its potential impact on performance. Furthermore, an ablation study on the distributed-to-centralized learning strategy would help justify its necessity.

In conclusion, while the paper presents a robust and innovative approach to collaborative SLAM, addressing the mentioned weaknesses and providing additional experiments will strengthen the paper's impact and clarity.

### References

[1] Campos, Carlos, et al. "ORB-SLAM3: An accurate open-source library for visual, visual–inertial, and multimap SLAM." IEEE Transactions on Robotics 37.6 (2021): 1874-1890.

[2] Tian, Yulun, et al. "Kimera-Multi: Robust, distributed, dense metric-semantic SLAM for multi-robot systems." IEEE Transactions on Robotics 38.4 (2022).

[3] Bian, Wenjing, et al. "NoPe-Nerf: Optimising neural radiance field with no pose prior." Proceedings of the IEEE/CVF Conference on Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition. 2023.

reviews.summary: There have been some papers on getting dense Visual SLAM with neural fields published in the last couple of years. In this work, the authors proposed a set of additions to prior pipelines that get the NN SLAM closer to the traditional approaches concerning the overall framework. Specifically, the authors addressed loop detection, pose graph optimization, bundle adjustment, and collaborative SLAM.
reviews.strengths:
1) Visual-SLAM with neural fields has the huge benefit of providing a dense map of the environment. The topic is relevant to computer vision, robotics, and I believe it fits the NeurIPS scope.
2) The paper is well-written, easy to follow, and well-organized. Related work is OK.
3) Although the technical novelties are relatively limited, in my opinion, the contributions to the pipeline are significant.
reviews.weaknesses:
1) As written above, the technical contributions are limited. The paper focuses more on adding new steps to prior Visual SLAM pipelines.
2) The inclusion of the NetVLAD (not new) as a tool for loop detection looks like a trick. It is not elegant, in the sense that it is a parallel feature extractor that (maybe) could be done using the neural field encoders.
3) Only one dataset and 4 sequences are used, which is small concerning quantity and variety.
4) I would prefer to see more comparisons with previous traditional methods (without neural fields).
5) Figures 5 and 6 are not easily readable.
reviews.questions:
1) I would like the authors to explain what is the motivation for the inclusion of NetVLAD. Can't those descriptors be obtained from the neural field?
2) It is written that a pre-trained NetVLAD was used. It would be important to give more details about this training and how it generalizes to different scenes.
3) Usually, with classical V-SLAM, the bundle adjustment estimates both the pose of the cameras and the 3D map simultaneously. It seems that the authors are doing this alternatively. I would like to see some comments on this.
4) Why are the results for Apartment 2 so bad for the proposed approach? I would like the authors to comment on this.
5) It seems that traditional V-SLAM methods using RGB-D are missing (such as OrbSLAM). I would like to see some comments on this.
6) Maybe I missed something. But it is not clear to me how is the error in Tab. 2 computed.
reviews.limitations: Limitations are adequately addressed.

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
There have been some papers on getting dense Visual SLAM with neural fields published in the last couple of years. In this work, the authors proposed a set of additions to prior pipelines that get the NN SLAM closer to the traditional approaches concerning the overall framework. Specifically, the authors addressed loop detection, pose graph optimization, bundle adjustment, and collaborative SLAM.

Strengths:
1) Visual-SLAM with neural fields has the huge benefit of providing a dense map of the environment. The topic is relevant to computer vision, robotics, and I believe it fits the NeurIPS scope.
2) The paper is well-written, easy to follow, and well-organized. Related work is OK.
3) Although the technical novelties are relatively limited, in my opinion, the contributions to the pipeline are significant.

Weaknesses:
1) As written above, the technical contributions are limited. The paper focuses more on adding new steps to prior Visual SLAM pipelines.
2) The inclusion of the NetVLAD (not new) as a tool for loop detection looks like a trick. It is not elegant, in the sense that it is a parallel feature extractor that (maybe) could be done using the neural field encoders.
3) Only one dataset and 4 sequences are used, which is small concerning quantity and variety.
4) I would prefer to see more comparisons with previous traditional methods (without neural fields).
5) Figures 5 and 6 are not easily readable.

Questions:
1) I would like the authors to explain what is the motivation for the inclusion of NetVLAD. Can't those descriptors be obtained from the neural field?
2) It is written that a pre-trained NetVLAD was used. It would be important to give more details about this training and how it generalizes to different scenes.
3) Usually, with classical V-SLAM, the bundle adjustment estimates both the pose of the cameras and the 3D map simultaneously. It seems that the authors are doing this alternatively. I would like to see some comments on this.
4) Why are the results for Apartment 2 so bad for the proposed approach? I would like the authors to comment on this.
5) It seems that traditional V-SLAM methods using RGB-D are missing (such as OrbSLAM). I would like to see some comments on this.
6) Maybe I missed something. But it is not clear to me how is the error in Tab. 2 computed.

Limitations:
Limitations are adequately addressed.

Rating:
5: Borderline accept: Technically solid paper where reasons to accept outweigh reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation. Please use sparingly.
reviews.text:
## Summary

The paper presents a collaborative implicit neural simultaneous localization and mapping (SLAM) system with RGB-D image sequences, named CP-SLAM. The system consists of complete front-end and back-end modules, including odometry, loop detection, sub-map fusion, and global refinement. The authors propose a novel neural point-based 3D scene representation, where each point maintains a learnable neural feature for scene encoding and is associated with a certain keyframe. A distributed-to-centralized learning strategy is also introduced to improve consistency and cooperation.

## Strengths

1. **Relevance and Organization**: The topic of dense Visual SLAM with neural fields is relevant to computer vision, robotics, and fits the NeurIPS scope. The paper is well-written, easy to follow, and well-organized, with a clear presentation of related work.

2. **Dense Mapping**: The use of neural fields provides a dense map of the environment, which is a significant benefit over traditional sparse methods.

## Weaknesses

1. **Technical Contributions**: The technical novelties are relatively limited, with the paper focusing more on adding new steps to prior Visual SLAM pipelines rather than introducing fundamentally new concepts.

2. **NetVLAD for Loop Detection**: The inclusion of NetVLAD as a tool for loop detection seems like a straightforward application rather than an elegant integration. It would be more desirable to derive descriptors directly from the neural field encoders.

3. **Limited Evaluation**: The evaluation is limited to a small number of sequences from a single dataset, which raises concerns about the generalizability of the results.

4. **Comparisons with Traditional Methods**: There is a lack of comparison with previous traditional methods that do not use neural fields, which would provide a more comprehensive understanding of the system's performance.

## Questions and Comments

1. **Motivation for NetVLAD**: The authors should explain the motivation for using NetVLAD. Could descriptors not be obtained directly from the neural field?

2. **Pre-trained NetVLAD**: More details about the pre-trained NetVLAD and its generalization to different scenes would be beneficial.

3. **Bundle Adjustment**: The authors mention doing bundle adjustment alternatively. Some comments on this approach and its implications would be helpful.

4. **Results for Apartment 2**: The authors should comment on why the results for Apartment 2 are comparatively worse.

5. **Traditional V-SLAM Methods**: The paper could benefit from comparisons with traditional V-SLAM methods using RGB-D, such as OrbSLAM.

6. **Error Computation**: Clarification on how the error in Tab. 2 is computed would be appreciated.

## Limitations

The limitations are adequately addressed, but further discussion on the system's scalability, robustness to various environments, and real-time performance would strengthen the paper.

## Rating

5: Borderline accept. The paper is technically solid, but reasons to accept outweigh reasons to reject due to limited evaluation. It is suggested to use this rating sparingly.

## Expanded Review

The paper proposes a collaborative implicit neural SLAM system, CP-SLAM, which integrates a novel neural point-based 3D scene representation and a distributed-to-centralized learning strategy. While the system shows promising results in dense mapping and collaborative SLAM, there are areas that require further clarification and improvement.

### Technical Contributions

The technical contributions, although significant in terms of pipeline additions, seem limited in terms of fundamental novelty. The authors primarily focus on integrating existing concepts into a more comprehensive framework. For instance, the use of NetVLAD for loop detection, while effective, does not represent a new technical contribution. Instead, it leverages an existing method (Relja Arandjelovic et al., 2016) to address a specific challenge in SLAM systems.

### Evaluation

The evaluation of CP-SLAM is based on a limited dataset, which includes only one dataset with four sequences. This limited evaluation raises concerns about the generalizability of the results across different environments and scenarios. To strengthen the paper, it would be beneficial to include more extensive evaluations, such as comparisons with state-of-the-art methods on multiple datasets.

### Comparison with Traditional Methods

The paper could benefit from more comparisons with traditional SLAM methods that do not use neural fields. This would provide a more comprehensive understanding of the system's performance and help to highlight its advantages and limitations. For example, comparisons with ORB-SLAM3 (Carlos Campos et al., 2021) and other traditional RGB-D SLAM methods would be insightful.

### NetVLAD for Loop Detection

The use of NetVLAD for loop detection, while effective, seems like a straightforward application rather than an elegant integration. It would be more desirable to derive descriptors directly from the neural field encoders, which could potentially lead to a more unified and efficient system.

### Bundle Adjustment

The authors mention doing bundle adjustment alternatively. Some comments on this approach and its implications would be helpful. Specifically, it would be interesting to discuss how this approach affects the system's accuracy and efficiency compared to traditional bundle adjustment methods.

### Results for Apartment 2

The results for Apartment 2 are comparatively worse, and the authors should comment on this. Understanding the reasons behind this performance discrepancy could provide valuable insights into the system's limitations and potential areas for improvement.

### Traditional V-SLAM Methods

The paper could benefit from comparisons with traditional V-SLAM methods using RGB-D, such as OrbSLAM. This would provide a more comprehensive understanding of the system's performance and help to highlight its advantages and limitations.

### Error Computation

Clarification on how the error in Tab. 2 is computed would be appreciated. Understanding the error computation method would help to ensure that the results are accurate and reliable.

### Limitations

The limitations are adequately addressed, but further discussion on the system's scalability, robustness to various environments, and real-time performance would strengthen the paper. Specifically, it would be interesting to discuss how the system handles large-scale environments, varying lighting conditions, and dynamic objects.

### Conclusion

In conclusion, while the paper presents a comprehensive and well-organized system for collaborative implicit neural SLAM, there are areas that require further clarification and improvement. Addressing these concerns and providing more extensive evaluations would strengthen the paper and make it more suitable for publication.

The final rating remains 5: Borderline accept. The paper is technically solid, but reasons to accept outweigh reasons to reject due to limited evaluation.

title: Learning Repeatable Speech Embeddings Using An Intra-class Correlation Regularizer

reviews.summary: Starting with the observation that supervised embeddings should vary from one class to another but not be sensitive to variations within a given class, this paper proposes to add an intra-class correlation (ICC) regularization to the contrastive loss in representation learning. This new regularizer is derived from measurement theory and encourages the embeddings to have a low intra-class variance, whereas contrastive losses pushes more the embeddings towards high inter-class variances. After a simulation illustrating the "behaviour" of these losses with respect to inter- and intra-class variance, the authors report good improvements using the proposed ICC regularizer for three different tasks.
reviews.strengths:
- The paper is overall well written, the idea well explained and easy to follow and to understand
  - The ICC regularizer seems to really focus on intra-class variance, i.e. make all occurences of the same class have the same embeddings, where contrastive loss does both at the same time and never really focuses on minimizing the intra-class variance.
  - The method is relatively simple, very well explained and easy to understand. The simulation, comparison with contrastive loss and classification error rate is quite interesting.
  - When the embeddings are constrained, it is indeed useful to focus on minimizing the intra-class variance to separate classes. The explanation and results are convincing.
reviews.weaknesses:
- Missing comparison with other techniques mentioned in the related work (data augmentation, etc.). Is the proposed approach competitive with these methods? Could it be complementary? How does it compare to VICReg [4] for example
  - In the result section, the text mainly lists the results in the tables. Maybe a reformulation would allow more space for example to study the effect of the regularization weight
reviews.questions:
Could that method apply to self-supervised approaches, where the notion of classes is less well defined?

Is it competitive with other methods mentioned at the beginning of the paper? Or could it be complementary?

Are the improvements with that method bigger on smaller datasets or are they independent of the dataset size? Is the method especially suited for some tasks and less for other (e.g. could that be useful for classification, or ASR?)?

minor remark:
  - sec 3.1: "represents the i-th samples" -> sample
reviews.limitations: yes
reviews.rating: 7: Accept: Technically solid paper, with high impact on at least one sub-area, or moderate-to-high impact on more than one areas, with good-to-excellent evaluation, resources, reproducibility, and no unaddressed ethical considerations.
reviews.confidence: 2: You are willing to defend your assessment, but it is quite likely that you did not understand the central parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work. Math/other details were not carefully checked.

reviews.text:
Summary:
Starting with the observation that supervised embeddings should vary from one class to another but not be sensitive to variations within a given class, this paper proposes to add an intra-class correlation (ICC) regularization to the contrastive loss in representation learning. This new regularizer is derived from measurement theory and encourages the embeddings to have a low intra-class variance, whereas contrastive losses push more the embeddings towards high inter-class variances. After a simulation illustrating the "behaviour" of these losses with respect to inter- and intra-class variance, the authors report good improvements using the proposed ICC regularizer for three different tasks.

The paper is well-structured and easy to follow, with a clear explanation of the proposed ICC regularizer and its benefits. The authors provide a thorough analysis of the ICC regularizer's performance on three speech tasks: speaker verification, voice style conversion, and dysphonic voice detection. The results demonstrate that the ICC regularizer can improve the repeatability of learned embeddings and lead to better performance in downstream tasks.

Strengths:
- The paper is overall well written, the idea is well explained and easy to follow and to understand. 
  The ICC regularizer seems to really focus on intra-class variance, i.e., making all occurrences of the same class have the same embeddings, where contrastive loss does both at the same time and never really focuses on minimizing the intra-class variance.
  - The method is relatively simple, very well explained, and easy to understand. The simulation, comparison with contrastive loss, and classification error rate is quite interesting.
  - When the embeddings are constrained, it is indeed useful to focus on minimizing the intra-class variance to separate classes. The explanation and results are convincing.

To further elaborate on the strengths, the ICC regularizer's focus on intra-class variance is a significant advantage over traditional contrastive losses. By minimizing the intra-class variance, the ICC regularizer ensures that embeddings from the same class are closely clustered together, which is essential for many downstream tasks. The authors' simulation study provides valuable insights into the behavior of the ICC regularizer and contrastive loss with respect to inter- and intra-class variance.

Weaknesses:
- Missing comparison with other techniques mentioned in the related work (data augmentation, etc.). Is the proposed approach competitive with these methods? Could it be complementary? How does it compare to VICReg [4] for example?
  - In the result section, the text mainly lists the results in the tables. Maybe a reformulation would allow more space for example to study the effect of the regularization weight.

One potential weakness of the paper is the lack of comparison with other techniques mentioned in the related work section. While the authors mention various methods for improving the robustness of learned latent representations, they do not provide a direct comparison with these methods. A comparison with techniques like data augmentation, VICReg, and other state-of-the-art methods would strengthen the paper and provide a more comprehensive understanding of the ICC regularizer's performance.

Questions:
Could that method apply to self-supervised approaches, where the notion of classes is less well defined?

Is it competitive with other methods mentioned at the beginning of the paper? Or could it be complementary?

Are the improvements with that method bigger on smaller datasets or are they independent of the dataset size? Is the method especially suited for some tasks and less for other (e.g., could that be useful for classification, or ASR?)?

The authors' proposal to use the ICC regularizer in self-supervised approaches is an interesting direction for future research. Self-supervised learning methods often rely on contrastive losses, and the ICC regularizer could potentially be used to improve the quality of the learned representations. Additionally, investigating the ICC regularizer's performance on smaller datasets and its applicability to various tasks, such as classification and ASR, would provide a more comprehensive understanding of its strengths and weaknesses.

minor remark:
  - sec 3.1: "represents the i-th samples" -> sample

The minor remark regarding the typo in section 3.1 is duly noted.

Limitations:
yes

The authors acknowledge the limitations of their work, including the need for hyperparameter fine-tuning when combining the ICC regularizer with contrastive loss. They also discuss the potential negative societal impact of their method, particularly in high-stakes applications like healthcare.

Rating:
7: Accept: Technically solid paper, with high impact on at least one sub-area, or moderate-to-high impact on more than one areas, with good-to-excellent evaluation, resources, reproducibility, and no unaddressed ethical considerations.

The expanded review supports the same acceptance/rejection recommendation as the original review. The paper is technically sound, and the proposed ICC regularizer has a significant impact on the field of representation learning. The authors provide a thorough evaluation of their method, and the results demonstrate its effectiveness in improving the repeatability of learned embeddings. While there are some limitations and potential areas for future research, the paper is well-written, and the ideas are well-explained and easy to follow.

In this paper, authors propose to use an intra-class correlation coefficient (ICC) regularizer as a complimentary component for contrastive loss to increase the repeatability of the supervised speech embedding. Authors conduct simulation for ICC regularizer and contrastive loss (GE2E loss) to explain their similarities and differences in the optimization criteria. Based on the experimental results on the speaker verification, voice style conversion, and a clinical application for detecting dysphonic three tasks, the authors claim that adding an ICC regularizer helps increase the repeatability and further improve the performance on downstream tasks.

Strengths:
1. This paper leverages the concept of repeatability and states that higher repeatability can contribute to improved performance in downstream tasks. Authors think about the importance of intra-class variance and the proposed ICC regularizer places greater emphasis on minimizing it to help increase repeatability. The simulation results in Section 3.2 explains the intuition clearly. 
 
2. As a complementary component to contrastive loss, adding the proposed ICC regularizer successfully increase the repeatability and shows good performance in the three different tasks.

Weaknesses:
1. In Task 1, the performance of SV system is not good though adding the ICC regularizer improves its performance. In this paper, VGG-M-40 is trained on VoxCeleb1 & 2. The best result (3.78% EER) reported in Table 1 is not as good as other commonly used models for SV. In Chung et al. paper, the VGG-M-40 shows much worse performance than Thin-ResNet34 and Fast ResNet34. To make the experimental solid and results and claims more convincible, authors should pick a good baseline instead of use the VGG-M-40.

4. Visualization (such as t-SNE) can be added as a comparison to see if the extracted speech or speaker embeddings have smaller intra-class variance in the space, though their ICC is decreased.

Questions:
1. As this paper mentioned, the ICC regularizer requires hyperparameter fine-tuning. Are the hyperparameter tuned on a validation set or a development set? 

2. As this paper mentioned in Section 3.2, though the ICC increased, do the learned embeddings are more compacted within-class and more separate between-class in the overall embedding space? Authors can use t-SNE to visualize them.

The authors do not analyze the potential negative societal impact.

reviews.text:
## Summary

In this paper, the authors propose to use an intra-class correlation coefficient (ICC) regularizer as a complementary component for contrastive loss to increase the repeatability of supervised speech embeddings. The authors conduct a simulation study to compare the ICC regularizer and contrastive loss (GE2E loss) and explain their similarities and differences in optimization criteria. Based on experimental results on three speech tasks - speaker verification, voice style conversion, and a clinical application for detecting dysphonic voice - the authors claim that adding an ICC regularizer helps increase repeatability and improves performance on downstream tasks.

## Strengths

1. This paper leverages the concept of repeatability and states that higher repeatability can contribute to improved performance in downstream tasks. The authors emphasize the importance of intra-class variance and propose the ICC regularizer, which places greater emphasis on minimizing it to help increase repeatability. The simulation results in Section 3.2 clearly explain the intuition behind this approach.

2. As a complementary component to contrastive loss, the proposed ICC regularizer successfully increases repeatability and shows good performance in the three different tasks.

## Weaknesses

1. In Task 1, the performance of the speaker verification (SV) system is not good, although adding the ICC regularizer improves its performance. The best result reported in Table 1 is 3.78% EER, which is not as good as other commonly used models for SV. For example, in Chung et al.'s paper, VGG-M-40 shows much worse performance than Thin-ResNet34 and Fast ResNet34. To make the experimental results solid and the conclusions more convincing, the authors should select a better baseline instead of using VGG-M-40.

2. For better reproducibility, more experiment details should be provided, such as the training details of Task 1.

3. To have comprehensive evaluation results for Task 1, the evaluation metric minDCF can be used as well.

4. Visualization (such as t-SNE) can be added as a comparison to see if the extracted speech or speaker embeddings have smaller intra-class variance in the space, though their ICC is decreased.

## Questions

1. As this paper mentioned, the ICC regularizer requires hyperparameter fine-tuning. Are the hyperparameters tuned on a validation set or a development set?

2. As this paper mentioned in Section 3.2, though the ICC increased, do the learned embeddings become more compact within-class and more separate between-class in the overall embedding space? The authors can use t-SNE to visualize them.

## Detailed Comments

The authors propose a novel approach to improve the repeatability of supervised speech embeddings by using an intra-class correlation coefficient (ICC) regularizer as a complementary component for contrastive loss. The ICC regularizer is designed to minimize the intra-class variance, which is essential for achieving high repeatability. The simulation study in Section 3.2 provides a clear explanation of the intuition behind the proposed approach.

However, there are some concerns regarding the experimental results. In Task 1, the performance of the SV system is not good, and the authors should select a better baseline instead of using VGG-M-40. Additionally, more experiment details should be provided, such as the training details of Task 1.

The authors also mention that the ICC regularizer requires hyperparameter fine-tuning. It would be helpful to provide more information about the hyperparameter tuning process, such as the range of values considered and the criteria used for selecting the optimal hyperparameters.

Furthermore, it would be interesting to see a visualization of the extracted speech or speaker embeddings to compare their intra-class variance in the space. This could provide additional insights into the effectiveness of the proposed approach.

## Limitations

The authors do not analyze the potential negative societal impact of their proposed approach. This is a crucial aspect that should be considered, especially in applications such as healthcare and speaker verification.

## Rating

6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.

## Expanded Review

The authors propose a novel approach to improve the repeatability of supervised speech embeddings by using an intra-class correlation coefficient (ICC) regularizer as a complementary component for contrastive loss. The ICC regularizer is designed to minimize the intra-class variance, which is essential for achieving high repeatability. The simulation study in Section 3.2 provides a clear explanation of the intuition behind the proposed approach.

The simulation study in Section 3.2 is based on a Monte Carlo simulation with 400 samples from an 8-dimensional, 4-class Gaussian mixture. The authors vary the intra-class variance and inter-class variance to generate samples and calculate the ICC regularizer value and GE2E loss value for these random samples. The results show that the ICC regularizer and GE2E loss have different contours, indicating that they have different optimization criteria.

The authors also conduct experiments on three speech tasks - speaker verification, voice style conversion, and a clinical application for detecting dysphonic voice. The results show that adding an ICC regularizer helps increase repeatability and improves performance on downstream tasks.

However, there are some concerns regarding the experimental results. In Task 1, the performance of the SV system is not good, and the authors should select a better baseline instead of using VGG-M-40. Additionally, more experiment details should be provided, such as the training details of Task 1.

The authors also mention that the ICC regularizer requires hyperparameter fine-tuning. It would be helpful to provide more information about the hyperparameter tuning process, such as the range of values considered and the criteria used for selecting the optimal hyperparameters.

Furthermore, it would be interesting to see a visualization of the extracted speech or speaker embeddings to compare their intra-class variance in the space. This could provide additional insights into the effectiveness of the proposed approach.

## Conclusion

In conclusion, the authors propose a novel approach to improve the repeatability of supervised speech embeddings by using an intra-class correlation coefficient (ICC) regularizer as a complementary component for contrastive loss. The simulation study and experimental results show that the proposed approach is effective in increasing repeatability and improving performance on downstream tasks. However, there are some concerns regarding the experimental results, and additional information about the hyperparameter tuning process and visualization of the extracted embeddings would be helpful.

## Recommendation

Based on the review, I recommend that the paper be accepted with revisions. The revisions should include:

* Selecting a better baseline for Task 1
* Providing more experiment details, such as the training details of Task 1
* Adding visualization of the extracted speech or speaker embeddings to compare their intra-class variance in the space
* Providing more information about the hyperparameter tuning process

The decision/rating remains exactly the same - 6: Weak Accept.

Intraclass Correlation (ICC) was proposed as an evaluation metric and the ICC regularizer was used as an additional element to contrastive loss for training. This aims to enhance the repeatability of deep-learned embeddings.
Some synthetic examples and intuitive reasoning were used to illustrate why the ICC regularizer is superior in minimizing intra-class variance compared to contrastive loss. The ICC regularizer was assessed across three speech tasks: speaker embeddings for Text-Independent Speaker Verification (TI-SV) and zero-shot voice style conversion, along with voice feature embeddings for a clinical application. The experiments indicate that the ICC regularizer can boost the repeatability of learned embeddings and perform better in downstream tasks.

reviews.text:
## Expanded Review

### Summary
The paper introduces the concept of repeatability from measurement theory for representation learning, proposing the Intraclass Correlation (ICC) as an evaluation metric and the ICC regularizer to enhance the repeatability of deep-learned embeddings. The ICC regularizer is assessed across three speech tasks: speaker embeddings for Text-Independent Speaker Verification (TI-SV) and zero-shot voice style conversion, along with voice feature embeddings for a clinical application. The experiments indicate that the ICC regularizer can boost the repeatability of learned embeddings and perform better in downstream tasks.

### Strengths
The paper proposes a novel metric of ICC based on measurement repeatability, and the motivation and intuition are well explained. The experimental results on three speaker/speech tasks are convincing.

### Weaknesses
To demonstrate the repeatability of speaker embeddings for the TI-SV task, more controlled experiments may be necessary. For instance, adding noise to the testing sample or switching languages of the same speaker, as tested in the NIST Speaker Recognition Evaluation, could help prove the effectiveness of the ICC loss. 

The comparison of results on dysphonic voice detection in Table 3 may be unfair, as baseline models (e.g., [18], [53], [22]) seem to be over-trained compared to the proposed model. It would be better to use quoted numbers from the original papers or train the models under the same conditions.

The paper lacks discussion on negative societal impact, which is an important aspect.

### Questions
See comments above in the weakness section.

### Limitations
The paper does not discuss potential negative societal impacts, such as the amplification of biases in speech recognition systems.

### Rating
6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.

### Detailed Review

The paper presents a well-structured approach to improving the repeatability of deep-learned embeddings using the ICC regularizer. The introduction of the ICC metric and its application to various speech tasks is convincing. However, to further strengthen the claims, additional experiments could be conducted to demonstrate the robustness of the ICC regularizer under various conditions.

For example, in the TI-SV task, the authors could test the speaker embeddings under noisy conditions or with varying speech content to evaluate their repeatability. This would help establish the effectiveness of the ICC regularizer in real-world scenarios.

Regarding the comparison with baseline models, it is essential to ensure a fair comparison. Either quote the numbers from the original papers or train the models under the same conditions to validate the superiority of the proposed method.

The paper's focus on repeatability is an interesting perspective, and the results are promising. However, addressing the mentioned weaknesses and limitations will further enhance the paper's quality and impact.

### Additional Suggestions
- Consider adding a discussion on the potential negative societal impacts of the proposed method.
- Provide more details on the experimental setup and hyperparameter tuning for the ICC regularizer.
- Explore the application of the ICC regularizer to other domains, such as computer vision or natural language processing.

### Conclusion
The paper presents a novel approach to improving the repeatability of deep-learned embeddings using the ICC regularizer. While the results are promising, additional experiments and discussions are necessary to strengthen the claims and address potential concerns. With revisions addressing these points, the paper has the potential to make a significant impact in the field of representation learning.
reviews.original_text:
Summary:
In this paper, the concept of repeatability from measurement theory was introduced for representation learning. Intraclass Correlation (ICC) was proposed as an evaluation metric and the ICC regularizer was used as an additional element to contrastive loss for training. This aims to enhance the repeatability of deep-learned embeddings.
Some synthetic examples and intuitive reasoning were used to illustrate why the ICC regularizer is superior in minimizing intra-class variance compared to contrastive loss. The ICC regularizer was assessed across three speech tasks: speaker embeddings for Text-Independent Speaker Verification (TI-SV) and zero-shot voice style conversion, along with voice feature embeddings for a clinical application. The experiments indicate that the ICC regularizer can boost the repeatability of learned embeddings and perform better in downstream tasks.

Strengths:
The paper proposed a novel metric of ICC based on measurement repeatability. Although the term may be a little confusing, the motivation 
and intuition were well explained. The experimental results on three speaker/speech tasks are convincing.

Weaknesses:
To show the repeatability of speaker embedding for TI-SV task, more controlled experiments may be necessary to prove the effectiveness of ICC loss in addition to the ICC scores: for example, adding noise to the testing sample, or switching languages of the same speaker as it was tested in the NIST Speaker Recognition Evaluation. 

For results on dysphonic voice detection in Table 3, other baseline models were definitely over-trained compared to the proposed model.
Why do you train your own models instead of quoting the numbers from the original papers? (see line 334-335). The results in Table 3 may be unfair comparisons.

Questions:
See the comments above in the weakness section.

Limitations:
No discussion on negative societal impact was included in the paper

Rating:
6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.

Summary:
The paper introduces the ICC regularizer, a novel regularization technique that complements the contrastive loss to enhance the repeatability of embeddings. The authors illustrate the reason why the ICC regularizer better minimizes the intra-class variance than the contrastive loss, offering a fresh perspective on latent representation learning. The experimental results demonstrate that the ICC regularizer enhances the repeatability of learned embeddings. Consequently, embeddings with higher repeatability exhibit superior performance in downstream tasks.

Strengths:
1. This paper links the concept of repeatability, derived from measurement theory, to deep-learned embeddings, which is novel and interesting. The authors present an explanation of how the ICC regularizer effectively reduces intra-class variance compared to the contrastive loss, offering a novel perspective on latent representation learning.
2. To enhance the repeatability of the learned embeddings, this paper introduces a novel regularizer called the ICC regularizer, which is used as a regularizer for the contrastive loss.
3. The experimental results indicate that using the ICC regularizer leading to better performance in three downstream tasks .

Weaknesses:
My main concerns are mainly related to the experiments. Please refer to the following Questions section for details.

Questions:
1. In Section 4.2, ''GE2E Loss + ICC Regularizer'' shows superior speech naturalness on subjective evaluations. However, the objective metrics like speaker similarity scores and word error rate (WER) are missing.
2. In Section 4.2, the paper aims at zero-shot voice style conversion, which requires highly generalized speaker embedding. However, the authors train and evaluate the zero-shot voice style conversion on a small dataset called VCTK. I would like to ask if the generalization performance of the proposed method be further improved by using larger training datasets? Additional experiments on larger dataset would make the paper more convincing.
3. Although the audio samples with ICC regularizer is better, after listening to the audio samples in the supplementary materials, I think the audio quality should be further improved for practical scenarios. I would like to ask is this due to the used AutoVC model? Could the authors use the latest SOTA voice conversion model to improve the speech quality?

I would like to raise my scores if the authors have fully addressed my concerns.

Limitations:
Some limitations of the work are discussed. The potential negative societal impact of the work is not addressed.

Rating:
5: Borderline accept: Technically solid paper where reasons to accept outweigh reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation. Please use sparingly.
reviews.text:
## Expanded Review

The paper introduces the ICC regularizer, a novel regularization technique that complements the contrastive loss to enhance the repeatability of embeddings. The authors illustrate the reason why the ICC regularizer better minimizes the intra-class variance than the contrastive loss, offering a fresh perspective on latent representation learning. The experimental results demonstrate that the ICC regularizer enhances the repeatability of learned embeddings. Consequently, embeddings with higher repeatability exhibit superior performance in downstream tasks.

## Strengths

1. This paper links the concept of repeatability, derived from measurement theory, to deep-learned embeddings, which is novel and interesting. The authors present an explanation of how the ICC regularizer effectively reduces intra-class variance compared to the contrastive loss, offering a novel perspective on latent representation learning.
2. To enhance the repeatability of the learned embeddings, this paper introduces a novel regularizer called the ICC regularizer, which is used as a regularizer for the contrastive loss.
3. The experimental results indicate that using the ICC regularizer leads to better performance in three downstream tasks.

## Weaknesses

The main concerns are related to the experiments. The authors should address the following questions:

1. **Objective metrics for zero-shot voice style conversion**: In Section 4.2, "GE2E Loss + ICC Regularizer" shows superior speech naturalness on subjective evaluations. However, objective metrics like speaker similarity scores and word error rate (WER) are missing. Adding these metrics would strengthen the evaluation.
2. **Generalization performance on larger datasets**: In Section 4.2, the paper aims at zero-shot voice style conversion, which requires highly generalized speaker embeddings. However, the authors train and evaluate the zero-shot voice style conversion on a relatively small dataset called VCTK. Using larger training datasets could potentially improve the generalization performance of the proposed method. Additional experiments on larger datasets would make the paper more convincing.
3. **Audio quality improvement**: Although the audio samples with ICC regularizer have better quality, the audio quality should be further improved for practical scenarios. This might be due to the used AutoVC model. Using the latest state-of-the-art voice conversion model could potentially improve the speech quality.

## Limitations

Some limitations of the work are discussed. However, the potential negative societal impact of the work remains unaddressed. It is essential to consider the potential risks and consequences of the proposed method, especially in high-stakes applications like healthcare, where biased predictions or decisions can lead to unequal treatment or access. Safe deployment of models based on the feature representations proposed herein will require thorough validation to detect potential biases and mitigation strategies for dealing with them.

## Decision and Rating

The decision remains **5: Borderline accept: Technically solid paper where reasons to accept outweigh reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation. Please use sparingly.**

The expanded review supports the same acceptance/rejection recommendation, preserving all original critique points and incorporating new observations and suggestions for improvement. The authors have a clear path to strengthen their paper by addressing the raised concerns and further evaluating their method on larger datasets and with more comprehensive metrics.

reviews.summary: This paper proposes to use a traditional intra-class correlation coefficient (ICC) to assess the repeatability of speech embeddings learnt by neural network. The proposed ICC regularizer has characteristics similar to well-known contrastive loss which aims to minimize the intra-class variance and maximize the inter-class variance of training data. The authors propose to use the ICC regularizer not in isolation but in combination with the contrastive loss. This paper performs several types of experiments based on speech processing tasks such as speaker verification, voice style conversion, and dysphonic voice detection to demonstrates the effectiveness of the proposed regularizer. These tasks focus on the importance of detecting the repeatability for the target data.
reviews.strengths:
- The considered scenario is interesting and important to the community. The proposed method is simple yet effective in assessing the repeatability in the target data.
- This paper assesses the effectiveness of the proposed method not only with a single speech task but also with several tasks based on voice quality evaluation. The improvements look sufficient.
reviews.weaknesses:
- ICC is very traditional metric, which is widely used for various data classification algorithms.
- The proposed ICC regularizer shouldn't be used in isolation.
- Lack of some experiments and details of experimental setup (see questions below).
reviews.questions:
- It looks like that the proposed ICC regularizer corresponds to the special case of contrastive loss focused only on positive sample pairs. Although there are many variants in contrastive loss, the basic concept is to increase a capability of category classification of data by leveraging both positive and negative pairs. I am wondering if the training only with conventional contrastive loss could provide some improvements similar to the proposed method, for example, by simply changing the ratio of positive sample pairs (reducing negative sample pairs) in the last half of the training stage. What are advantages that cannot be obtained by just changing the ratio of positive and negative pair data?
- It is difficult to understand the effectiveness of the proposed method correctly because experimental setups including data are very briefly written. For example, in the experiment described in Section 4.1, what are the training strategy of the model such as an optimizer, a learning rate scheduling, other regularization techniques (specAugment, DropConnect, etc.), data augmentation, and so on?
- In addition, a summary of data used for each experiment (a few more details) would be very much helpful.
reviews.limitations: The same comments as one of weaknesses. The proposed ICC regularizer shouldn't be used in isolation but be used in combination with a conventional contrastive loss as clearly mentioned in the paper.

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This paper proposes to use a traditional intra-class correlation coefficient (ICC) to assess the repeatability of speech embeddings learnt by neural network. The proposed ICC regularizer has characteristics similar to well-known contrastive loss which aims to minimize the intra-class variance and maximize the inter-class variance of training data. The authors propose to use the ICC regularizer not in isolation but in combination with the contrastive loss. This paper performs several types of experiments based on speech processing tasks such as speaker verification, voice style conversion, and dysphonic voice detection to demonstrates the effectiveness of the proposed regularizer. These tasks focus on the importance of detecting the repeatability for the target data.

Strengths:
- The considered scenario is interesting and important to the community. The proposed method is simple yet effective in assessing the repeatability in the target data.
- This paper assesses the effectiveness of the proposed method not only with a single speech task but also with several tasks based on voice quality evaluation. The improvements look sufficient.

Weaknesses:
- ICC is very traditional metric, which is widely used for various data classification algorithms.
- The proposed ICC regularizer shouldn't be used in isolation.
- Lack of some experiments and details of experimental setup (see questions below).

Questions:
- It looks like that the proposed ICC regularizer corresponds to the special case of contrastive loss focused only on positive sample pairs. Although there are many variants in contrastive loss, the basic concept is to increase a capability of category classification of data by leveraging both positive and negative pairs. I am wondering if the training only with conventional contrastive loss could provide some improvements similar to the proposed method, for example, by simply changing the ratio of positive sample pairs (reducing negative sample pairs) in the last half of the training stage. What are advantages that cannot be obtained by just changing the ratio of positive and negative pair data?
- It is difficult to understand the effectiveness of the proposed method correctly because experimental setups including data are very briefly written. For example, in the experiment described in Section 4.1, what are the training strategy of the model such as an optimizer, a learning rate scheduling, other regularization techniques (specAugment, DropConnect, etc.), data augmentation, and so on?
- In addition, a summary of data used for each experiment (a few more details) would be very much helpful.

Limitations:
The same comments as one of weaknesses. The proposed ICC regularizer shouldn't be used in isolation but be used in combination with a conventional contrastive loss as clearly mentioned in the paper.

Rating:
5: Borderline accept: Technically solid paper where reasons to accept outweigh reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation. Please use sparingly.

Summary:
This paper proposes to use a traditional intra-class correlation coefficient (ICC) to assess the repeatability of speech embeddings learnt by neural network. The proposed ICC regularizer has characteristics similar to well-known contrastive loss which aims to minimize the intra-class variance and maximize the inter-class variance of training data. The authors propose to use the ICC regularizer not in isolation but in combination with the contrastive loss. This paper performs several types of experiments based on speech processing tasks such as speaker verification, voice style conversion, and dysphonic voice detection to demonstrates the effectiveness of the proposed regularizer. These tasks focus on the importance of detecting the repeatability for the target data.

Strengths:
- The considered scenario is interesting and important to the community. The proposed method is simple yet effective in assessing the repeatability in the target data.
- This paper assesses the effectiveness of the proposed method not only with a single speech task but also with several tasks based on voice quality evaluation. The improvements look sufficient.

Weaknesses:
- ICC is very traditional metric, which is widely used for various data classification algorithms.
- The proposed ICC regularizer shouldn't be used in isolation.
- Lack of some experiments and details of experimental setup (see questions below).

Questions:
- It looks like that the proposed ICC regularizer corresponds to the special case of contrastive loss focused only on positive sample pairs. Although there are many variants in contrastive loss, the basic concept is to increase a capability of category classification of data by leveraging both positive and negative pairs. I am wondering if the training only with conventional contrastive loss could provide some improvements similar to the proposed method, for example, by simply changing the ratio of positive sample pairs (reducing negative sample pairs) in the last half of the training stage. What are advantages that cannot be obtained by just changing the ratio of positive and negative pair data?
- It is difficult to understand the effectiveness of the proposed method correctly because experimental setups including data are very briefly written. For example, in the experiment described in Section 4.1, what are the training strategy of the model such as an optimizer, a learning rate scheduling, other regularization techniques (specAugment, DropConnect, etc.), data augmentation, and so on?
- In addition, a summary of data used for each experiment (a few more details) would be very much helpful.

Limitations:
The same comments as one of weaknesses. The proposed ICC regularizer shouldn't be used in isolation but be used in combination with a conventional contrastive loss as clearly mentioned in the paper.

5: Borderline accept: Technically solid paper where reasons to accept outweigh reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation. Please use sparingly.

title: PromptRestorer: A Prompting Image Restoration Method with Degradation Perception

reviews.summary: This paper proposes a prompting image restoration method with degradation perception. The authors show that raw degradation features can effectively guide deep restoration models, providing accurate degradation priors to facilitate better restoration. To perceive the degradation, the authors propose prompting degradation perception modulator. To control the propagation of the perceived content for restoration branch, the authors propose gated degradation perception propagation.
reviews.strengths:
+ This paper is professionally wrote and presents a better organization.
+ The proposed prompting image restoration method is interesting, which clearly demonstrate that the raw degradation features are better guidance than the learnable parameters used in previous methods.
+ The proposed prompting degradation perception modulator is novel. It has potential applications to latter features fusion manner.
+ The gated degradation perception propagation seems to be useful.
reviews.weaknesses:
1, The author claim that the pre-trained model is the same with the learnable conditional network. Hence, the FLOPs in Tab. 5 should be same.

2, The authors would better to explain why the raw degradation features are better than features from learnable parameters network since more learnable parameters should have better results within the similar network framework.
3, SFT is a commonly used feature fusion module, is the proposed PromptDPM than that? What is the result if replacing the PromptDPM with SFT?
reviews.questions: see weakness
reviews.limitations: see weakness
reviews.rating: 7: Accept: Technically solid paper, with high impact on at least one sub-area, or moderate-to-high impact on more than one areas, with good-to-excellent evaluation, resources, reproducibility, and no unaddressed ethical considerations.

Summary:
This paper proposes a prompting image restoration method with degradation perception. The authors show that raw degradation features can effectively guide deep restoration models, providing accurate degradation priors to facilitate better restoration. To perceive the degradation, the authors propose prompting degradation perception modulator. To control the propagation of the perceived content for restoration branch, the authors propose gated degradation perception propagation.

Strengths:
+ This paper is professionally wrote and presents a better organization.
+ The proposed prompting image restoration method is interesting, which clearly demonstrate that the raw degradation features are better guidance than the learnable parameters used in previous methods.
+ The proposed prompting degradation perception modulator is novel. It has potential applications to latter features fusion manner.
+ The gated degradation perception propagation seems to be useful.

Weaknesses:
1, The author claim that the pre-trained model is the same with the learnable conditional network. Hence, the FLOPs in Tab. 5 should be same.

2, The authors would better to explain why the raw degradation features are better than features from learnable parameters network since more learnable parameters should have better results within the similar network framework.
3, SFT is a commonly used feature fusion module, is the proposed PromptDPM than that? What is the result if replacing the PromptDPM with SFT?

Questions:
see weakness

Limitations:
see weakness
